title: DNAzyme-like Motifs, Horizontal Gene Transfer, and Short Linear Motif Conservation in Potyviridae
text:
Project Hail Mary
DNAzyme-like motifs, horizontal gene transfer, and short linear motif conservation in Potyviridae

title: DNAzyme-like Motifs, Horizontal Gene Transfer, and Short Linear Motif Conservation in Potyviridae
text:
Overview
This compendium documents the discovery and characterisation of a DNAzyme-like catalytic domain sequence that maps precisely to the genomes of multiple Potyviridae species, with flanking regions matching eukaryotic genes across plant, fungal, and animal kingdoms. Cross-kingdom horizontal gene transfer is the most parsimonious explanation for this pattern. Additionally, the Potyviridae CI helicase - harbouring the DNAzyme-like sequence, corresponds to a conserved protein motif likely involved in viral-host interactions associated with intracellular sorting (potentially via AP-4), viral replication, viral spread and immunity.

Key findings at a glance

A DNAzyme catalytic domain sequence matches perfectly to a region in the genomes of several potyviruses (6 species, 16 isolates).
The flanking regions of those DNAzyme-matching sequences (16 isolates, 9 identical by sequence) are identical to regions in the genomes of several eukaryotes, including plants (potential viral hosts) and insects (potential viral vectors).
Interactions between those eukaryotic species suggest the homology might reflect cross-kingdom horizontal DNA transfer.
The eukaryotic genes span diverse functions — DNA repair, transcription regulation, chromatin remodelling, RNA processing, translation, and genes of unknown function — several of them essential for cell survival and development.

The work spans several interconnected research threads:

Reproducibility
All computationally intensive steps (alignment, phylogenetic inference, NCBI fetching, ELM queries) are orchestrated by a targets pipeline:
# Install the pipeline dependencies once
install.packages(c("targets", "tarchetypes"))
remotes::install_local("packages/potytools")

# Run the full pipeline (skips up-to-date steps automatically)
targets::tar_make()

# Visualise dependencies
targets::tar_visnetwork()
External tools required on PATH for running the entire pipeline: mafft, iqtree2, hmmscan.

title: 1  The DNAzyme-like Motif Discovery
text:
1.1 Rationale and roadmap
To make the reasoning followable — and to be explicit about where it is speculation versus supported — here is the actual chain of thought, step by step:
Where this goes. Assessing potential DNAzyme catalytic activity (Chapter 3) shows most homologs carry substitutions at activity-critical core positions, so DNAzyme activity is unlikely / rare — and would additionally require reverse transcription to coincide. The nucleotide-level analyses (logos, GC/CpG, codon usage) are therefore exploratory context, not evidence. The thread that does lead somewhere is the CI protein itself — specifically host-type sorting motifs in the DNAzyme-locus region (a tyrosine-based motif beside an acidic dileucine signal; Chapter 5) — which connects to AP-4-dependent trafficking and the LE/MVB and motivates the immune-trafficking hypothesis of Chapter 9.

title: 1  The DNAzyme-like Motif Discovery
text:
Start from a real DNAzyme. I BLASTed the catalytic core of the 10-23 deoxyribozyme (Borggräfe et al. 2022) against the viral nucleotide collection and recovered 16 potyvirus isolates (6 species) carrying that core.
Ask whether it could be active. If the core were catalytically active, it would cleave whatever RNA is complementary to the two flanking oligomers (the arms that give a 10-23 DNAzyme its target specificity). So I searched eukaryotic genomes for perfect matches to the 9 + 9 nt flanks of all 16 isolates — i.e. for the sequences whose transcripts a natural version of this DNAzyme might target.
The flank-matches hinted at an ecological pattern. A quick look at two flanks (from a Turnip mosaic virus and a Maize dwarf mosaic virus isolate) suggested the recovered species might be ecologically associated, which made me speculate the viral↔︎eukaryote matches could be signatures of horizontal gene transfer (HGT) to vectors and/or hosts.
Caveat — this is not yet established. It is not shown that the flank-matching species actually are hosts or vectors of the corresponding potyvirus. I built the BioNexus app (Chapter 7) to test connectivity; the interactions looked sporadic rather than continuous — which is not enough to infer the absence of HGT either.
A small aside. A central target nucleotide was initially omitted; adding it back (as G) gives exactly one isolate a single perfect match to Aphis gossypii (a principal aphid vector) — a nice, suggestive observation, not strong evidence.
The locus is the CI gene. The DNAzyme-like sequence maps to the coding region of the potyviral cylindrical inclusion (CI) protein (Chapters 2–4).

title: 1  The DNAzyme-like Motif Discovery
text:
1.2 Intro
The starting point is a synthetic 10-23 deoxyribozyme (Borggräfe et al. 2022): a single-stranded DNA able to cleave matching single-stranded RNA. The central catalytic domain of the DNAzyme comprises 15 nt - which are flanked by at least 9 nt 3’ and 5’. The flanks, in turn represent the substrate-binding arms, i.e. they determine the specificity of the DNAzyme towards the RNA it is able to cleave. Complementary base-pairing with target RNA (Watson–Crick), positions the 15-nt catalytic core that cleaves the RNA at an unpaired purine·pyrimidine junction.
  target RNA   3'– A A C C C C A U U | C C A C G U A C A –5'
                   | | | | | | | | |   | | | | | | | | |
  DNAzyme      5'– T T G G G G T A A   G G T G C A T G T –3'
                     binding arm       binding arm
                 catalytic core: 5'– G G C T A G C T A C A A C G A –3'
The RNA is cleaved at the unpaired junction (|) between the two arms. The 15-nt catalytic core GGCTAGCTACAACGA — the part with no base-pairing role in recognition — is the sequence that turns out to match the potyvirus CI coding region (below). Scheme redrawn after Borggräfe et al. 2022 (Borggräfe et al. 2022); no figure is reproduced.

title: 1  The DNAzyme-like Motif Discovery
section: 2.1 Blasting the catalytic center of a DNAzyme
text:
2.1 Blasting the catalytic center of a DNAzyme
I had blasted the catalytic domain of the 10-23 DNAzyme characterised by Borggräfe et al. (2022).
I was curious, whether they (DNAzyme(s)) might exist or have existed in nature. That’s also why I initially screened the experimental virus database (as opposed to picking one for “higher” organisms) available in NCBI blast. I set the search parameters, so that it worked with short input sequences, not allowing any mismatches or gaps (blast will adjust some settings accordingly when the query is short).

2.1.0.1 Blast Version:
BLASTN 2.17.0+


2.1.0.2 BlastSearchID (RID):
SHEST00P014


2.1.0.3 Blast Reference:
The BLAST algorithm and scoring are described in Altschul et al. (1997).

As my query was a short sequence (15mer) and the database is far from small, I had naively expected that I should get some hits by chance alone already, which I suspected could be more or less randomly distributed over a couple of possibly very different (i.e. not necessarily closely related) species.
The search retrieved 16 isolates assigned to a total of 6 species, all belonging to the same genus: Potyvirus. That’s where I almost cut my fingers with Occam’s Razor: Potyviruses are members of the family of Potyviridae, which in turn classify as Type IV group viruses according to the Baltimore classification (i.e. they are +ssRNA viruses). DNAzyme activity therefore is nothing, that could be expected directly.

2.2.2 The fun part…
I then extracted and then blasted the 9+9bp flanking regions of the catalytic domain(s) (i.e. the 18bp sequence that is matching the viral genomes). This time I did not blast against the viral database. Instead, I went for the eukaryote nucleotide collection in NCBI and found that it matches perfectly to a region in the genomes of several eukaryotes.

title: 1  The DNAzyme-like Motif Discovery
text:

lapply(potymatchflanksls, width)

2.3.2 Sequence logo of viral fragments
First, I check whether there is any conservation in the viral sequences beyond the putative catalytic domains, using a seqlogo generated from the extended-flank segments (25bp flank + 15bp catalytic center + 25bp flank) of all 16 viral genomes.


Code
pfm <- consensusMatrix(potyseqsls$`25bp`)




Code
seqlogo <- ggseqlogo(pfm, seq_type = 'dna')
seqlogo





Sequence logo of the 25-nt DNAzyme-matching region across Potyviridae isolates; letter height is information content (bits), so tall stacks mark near-invariant positions.






Code
ggplot() +
  annotate('rect', xmin = 25.5, xmax = 40.5, ymin = -0.05, ymax = 2.05,
           alpha = .5, col = 'black', fill = 'grey') +
  geom_logo(pfm, stack_width = 0.90, seq_type = "dna") +
  annotate('text', x = 33, xmax = 40.5, y = 2.2, label = 'catalytic15') +
  theme_logo()





The same region with the 15-nt 10-23 catalytic core highlighted (grey box, positions 26-40); the core is the most conserved stretch of the alignment.






2.3.3 FunFacts
Plant viruses: “Plant viruses are grouped into 73 genera and 49 families. However, these figures relate only to cultivated plants, which represent only a tiny fraction of the total number of plant species.”
On the CI protein (which is where the DNAzyme motif often seems to be located): “CI (~71 kDa) is an RNA helicase with ATPase activity. Its most unusual property is its ability to form large and highly symmetrical conical and cylindrical inclusions with a central hollow cylinder from which laminate sheets radiate outward and fold in on themselves in a pattern often described as ‘pinwheels’.”

extract_ncbi_coords <- function(x) {
  nms <- if (methods::is(x, "XStringSet")) names(x) else x
  pattern <- "([A-Z0-9_]+\\.[0-9]+)\\.([+-]):(-?[0-9]+)-(-?[0-9]+)"
  m <- regexec(pattern, nms)
  matches <- regmatches(nms, m)
  do.call(rbind, lapply(matches, function(x) {
    if (length(x) == 5) {
      data.frame(full_name = x[1], seqname = x[2], strand = x[3],
                 start = as.integer(x[4]), end = as.integer(x[5]),
                 stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
    } else {
      data.frame(full_name = paste0(x), seqname = NA, strand = NA,
                 start = NA, end = NA, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
    }
  }))
}

extract_ncbi_granges <- function(x) {
  df <- extract_ncbi_coords(x)
  GenomicRanges::makeGRangesFromDataFrame(df, keep.extra.columns = TRUE)
}

library(GenomicRanges)
library(IRanges)

biostring_to_granges <- function(dna) {
  if (!methods::is(dna, "XStringSet"))
    stop("Input must be a DNAStringSet or other XStringSet")
  df <- extract_ncbi_coords(dna)
  gr <- GRanges(
    seqnames = df$seqname,
    ranges   = IRanges(start = df$start, end = df$end),
    strand   = df$strand)
  mcols(gr)$sequence <- dna
  gr
}

3.2.1 Implications part I
Now the crazy part. For these two examples (sequences based on a) a Turnip mosaic virus and b) a maize dwarf something virus, the flanking regions appeared to occur MASSIVELY enriched in very specific clades:

Plants.

The fact that the viruses appear to leave traces in the genomes of putative hosts is pretty “wow” already. These viruses are no retroviruses, which in turn are indeed known to insert their genomes or parts of it in that of their hosts. Secondly, because it is not a continuous part of the viral genome, but specifically one that matches a DNAzyme TARGET only (without the catalytic loop thingy in the middle, that still is present in the virus).
Another thing I think is interesting is that when I searched the Turnip virus sequences, I did not find ANY monocotyledons (Turnip is a dicotyledonous plant). When I tried with the Maize flanks, the hits however did indeed include monocotyledonous plants. (And wine btw, and cassava).

Insects

But not just any insects. Pollinators!!!!
This is not only indicating that DNAzymes might have existed in nature, but also that they might have been transferred between species and even kingdoms. Here’s the origin of life … right in front of us.

Some of the annotated hits include:

MIZU-KUSSEI 1: involved in hydrotropism and induced by ABA. Related to chromatin remodelling and potentially hypomethylation, which might trigger transposon activity.
myosin-VIIa-like: motor protein.
SH3 domain protein: PI3 Kinase, Ras GTPase-activating protein, CDC24 signalling.
calcineurin binding protein 1: CAIN — negative regulator of NFAT-dependent signalling.
ATP-dependent RNA helicase DHX40: associated with colorectal and haematological neoplasia.
leucine rich repeat domain containing protein: effector signalling, innate immunity.
DEAH box helicase 9: plays a role in HIV, binds RISC loading complex.

Sounds a lot like immune-related stuff. Update months later: Of course it does resemble some helicase — the sequence is part of CI, which is a helicase. Somewhat expected.

title: 1  The DNAzyme-like Motif Discovery
text:
3.3 Chromosomal positions of blast hits


Code
plotdatbhits <- hits %>%
  dplyr::mutate(relative = end/acc_len) %>%
  dplyr::select(c(acc_len, start, end, seqname, name, Description, query_acc, relative))

ggplot(plotdatbhits, mapping = aes(x = relative, y = name, color = name)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(), legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(query_acc~., scales = "free_y")





Relative chromosomal position (hit end / accession length) of each eukaryotic BLAST hit, one point per hit, faceted by query. Consistent positioning across hosts would indicate the flanks map to defined host loci rather than dispersed repeats.






Code
ggplot(plotdatbhits, mapping = aes(x = relative)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank()) +
  facet_wrap(query_acc~., scales = "free_y")





Density of the same BLAST hits along the relative chromosomal position, faceted by query — summarising where on their host chromosomes the flanking-region homologs concentrate.

oops    <- list.files(here::here("data"), pattern = "rc_G0_Blasthits.txt", full.names = TRUE)
oopsaln <- read_delim(oops, delim = "\t")

rc_G <- oopsaln %>%
  dplyr::mutate(query_cover_num = as.numeric(stringr::str_remove(query_cover, "%"))) %>%
  dplyr::filter(query_cover_num >= 99)

rc_G %>% ggplot(aes(y = query)) + geom_bar() + theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "number of identical blast hits", y = "")





Number of near-identical BLAST hits (≥99 % query coverage) per query flank, quantifying how widely each reverse-complement flank is shared across eukaryotic genomes.






Code
newtree  <- read.tree(here::here("data", "phyliptreeunex.phy"))
newtreef <- fortify(newtree)
ftdl2    <- ggtree(as.phylo(newtreef), layout = "daylight")

labelinodes <- c("Hexapoda", "Arthropoda", "Neoptera", "Embryophyta")

df <- newtreef %>%
  dplyr::mutate(labelinodes = case_when(
    newtreef$label %in% labelinodes ~ paste0(label),
    TRUE ~ NA))

rc_G$species <- gsub(" $", "", rc_G$species)
rc2G <- rc_G %>% dplyr::mutate(label = paste0("'", species, "'"))

df2 <- left_join(df, rc2G) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(virus = case_when(
    str_detect(query, "Maize")   ~ "Maize dwarf mosaic",
    str_detect(query, "Leek")    ~ "Leek yellow stripe",
    str_detect(query, "potato")  ~ "Sweet potato f.m.",
    str_detect(query, "Ocimum")  ~ "Basil putative",
    str_detect(query, "Turnip")  ~ "Turnip mosaic virus"))

ggtree(df2) + geom_tiplab() +
  geom_nodelab(mapping = aes(label = labelinodes)) +
  geom_tippoint(mapping = aes(color = virus)) +
  xlim(0, 150) + ylim(0, 60)





Neighbour-joining tree of the eukaryotic species bearing the flanking-region hits; internal nodes labelled with major clades (Embryophyta, Arthropoda, …) and tips coloured by the associated potyvirus, showing the cross-kingdom spread of the flanks.





3.4.1 Some more?
The 10-23 deoxyribozyme has been structurally characterised in a homodimer conformation (Cramer et al. 2023). Its target / catalytic-core arrangement is:




5’
cat
3’




Target

a



DNAzyme
gcacaatg
ggctagctacaacga
atccccag



The related 8-17 DNAzyme can operate in a single active structure regardless of the metal-ion cofactor (Wieruszewska et al. 2024). There the catalytic domain corresponds to 5’ GCCAGCGCCTCGAA 3’, and the target harbours an additional nucleotide between the specificity-determining flanks.

title: 1  The DNAzyme-like Motif Discovery
text:
3.5 Open Questions, Outlook, Thoughts
Is this the only example? What is a computationally reasonable way to find analogous events and potentially their viral/bacterial/molecular sources?

3.5.1 Statistics
Likelihoods:

What is the likelihood of finding any random 18mer in a database of a given size?
What is the expected distribution of hits for random 18mers among different groups within the database?
Can I pinpoint the time of insertion of the viral sequence into the host genome?



3.5.2 The RNA argument

“but the virus is RNA!!?”

An RNA genome is not an obstacle: genome sequences of non-retroviral RNA viruses are widely endogenized into plant genomes (Chiba et al. 2011), providing a precedent for the DNA-level integration of a (+)-strand RNA virus sequence proposed here. As those authors put it:

“The pattern of occurrence of NRVSs and the phylogenetic analyses of NRVSs and related viruses indicate that multiple independent integrations into many plant lineages may have occurred.”
— Chiba et al. (2011)

title: 2  Potyvirus Phylogenetics and CI Region Inference
text:
3 Introduction
This chapter performs a comprehensive phylogenetic pipeline for potyviruses, without the Bioconductor genbankr package. It reads a MAFFT protein alignment, separates references and isolates, retrieves reference CDS via rentrez, parses CDS from plain GenBank flatfiles, projects to codon alignment, infers the CI region automatically, extracts a short motif of interest, visualises these motifs on the resulting tree, and tests for convergent evolution versus shared ancestry.


4 Setup and Helpers
GenBank parsing, accession extraction, CDS-matching, codon projection and safe translation now live in potytools (extract_accessions(), has_genbank_id(), parse_genbank_file(), match_cds_to_reference(), codon_align_from_protein(), write_iqtree_codon_partitions(), safe_translate()) — see packages/potytools/R/parse_genbank.R and codon_analysis.R. The inline versions previously here had two correctness bugs the package versions fix: they stripped IUPAC ambiguity codes (e.g. N) from the genome before applying position-based CDS coordinates, silently frame-shifting any genome containing one; and they didn’t strip </> partial-location markers before as.integer(), corrupting old-style multi-CDS annotations. Validated against all 219 cached GenBank flatfiles: the package’s match_cds_to_reference() reproduces every affected record’s own /translation= qualifier exactly.


5 Data Input


Code
prot_file <- here::here("data", "OPSR.Poty.Fig3.v16_align_withblasthits_protmafftadd.txt")
orf_file  <- here::here("data", "isolates_longest_orfs.fna")
cache_dir <- here::here("cache", "genbank")

prot_aln <- readAAStringSet(prot_file)
stopifnot(length(unique(width(prot_aln))) == 1)
cat("Protein alignment loaded: length =", width(prot_aln)[1], "aa\n")


Protein alignment loaded: length = 6619 aa




6 Reference and Isolate Separation


Code
ref_idx  <- has_genbank_id(names(prot_aln))
prot_ref <- prot_aln[ref_idx]
prot_orf <- prot_aln[!ref_idx]
cat(length(prot_ref), "reference sequences and", length(prot_orf), "isolate sequences.\n")


219 reference sequences and 0 isolate sequences.




7 Retrieve Reference CDS
This section downloads GenBank flatfiles for references and extracts the polyprotein CDS via our parser.


Code
# Requires internet access + rentrez; run manually and cache results to data/
dir.create(cache_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
cds_ref  <- DNAStringSet()
ref_diag <- tibble(name = names(prot_ref), status = NA_character_, note = NA_character_)

for (i in seq_along(prot_ref)) {
  nm <- names(prot_ref)[i]; accs <- extract_accessions(nm)
  found <- FALSE
  for (acc in accs) {
    gb_file <- file.path(cache_dir, paste0(acc, ".gb"))
    if (!file.exists(gb_file)) {
      try({
        gb_txt <- entrez_fetch("nuccore", acc, rettype = "gb", retmode = "text")
        writeLines(gb_txt, gb_file)
      }, silent = TRUE)
    }
    if (!file.exists(gb_file)) next
    rec <- parse_genbank_file(gb_file)[[1]]
    ref_protein <- gsub("-", "", as.character(prot_ref[[nm]]))
    cds_seq <- match_cds_to_reference(rec, name = nm, ref_protein = ref_protein)
    if (is.null(cds_seq)) next
    if (length(cds_seq) %% 3 != 0)
      cds_seq <- subseq(cds_seq, start = 1, end = length(cds_seq) - (length(cds_seq) %% 3))
    if (length(cds_seq) == 0) next
    cds_ref[[nm]] <- cds_seq
    ref_diag$status[i] <- "ok"
    ref_diag$note[i]   <- acc
    found <- TRUE
    break
  }
  if (!found) {
    ref_diag$status[i] <- "failed"; ref_diag$note[i] <- "no CDS"
  }
}




8 Read Isolate CDS


Code
cds_orf <- readDNAStringSet(orf_file)
cds_orf <- DNAStringSet(lapply(cds_orf, function(x) {
  len <- length(x)
  if (len %% 3 != 0) x <- subseq(x, start = 1, end = len - (len %% 3))
  x
}))




9 Merge and Project to Codon Alignment


Code
cds_all  <- c(cds_ref, cds_orf)
keep_ids <- intersect(names(prot_aln), names(cds_all))
codon_aln <- codon_align_from_protein(prot_aln[keep_ids], cds_all)
stopifnot(length(unique(width(codon_aln))) == 1)
writeXStringSet(codon_aln, here::here("data", "potyvirus_codon_alignment.fna"))
cat("Codon alignment constructed: length =", width(codon_aln)[1], "nt\n")




10 Partition Files for IQ-TREE


Code
write_iqtree_codon_partitions(codon_aln,
  file = here::here("data", "iqtree_codon_partitions.txt"))

potyvirus_subproteins <- tibble(
  protein  = c("NIb", "CP"),
  aa_start = c(4100, 5600),
  aa_end   = c(5600, 6619)
) %>% mutate(nt_start = (aa_start - 1) * 3 + 1, nt_end = aa_end * 3)

lines_sub <- c()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(potyvirus_subproteins))) {
  p <- potyvirus_subproteins$protein[i]
  s <- potyvirus_subproteins$nt_start[i]
  e <- potyvirus_subproteins$nt_end[i]
  lines_sub <- c(lines_sub,
    sprintf("DNA, %s_codon1 = %d-%d\\3", p, s, e),
    sprintf("DNA, %s_codon2 = %d-%d\\3", p, s + 1, e),
    sprintf("DNA, %s_codon3 = %d-%d\\3", p, s + 2, e))
}
writeLines(lines_sub, here::here("data", "iqtree_subprotein_codon_partitions.txt"))




11 Automatic CI Coordinates Inference
We infer the CI region from GenBank annotations where available and provide a fallback region.


Code
ci_start <- NA; ci_end <- NA
for (nm in names(prot_ref)) {
  accs <- extract_accessions(nm)
  if (length(accs) == 0) next
  gb_file <- file.path(cache_dir, paste0(accs[1], ".gb"))
  if (!file.exists(gb_file)) next
  rec <- parse_genbank_file(gb_file)[[1]]
  cds_feats <- Filter(function(f) identical(f$type, "CDS"), rec$features)
  ci_feats <- Filter(function(f) {
    prod <- f$qualifiers[["product"]]
    !is.null(prod) && grepl("cylindrical inclusion|\\bci\\b", tolower(prod[1]))
  }, cds_feats)
  if (length(ci_feats) > 0) {
    ci_start <- ci_feats[[1]]$start / 3
    ci_end   <- ci_feats[[1]]$end / 3
    break
  }
}
if (is.na(ci_start) || is.na(ci_end)) {
  aa_len   <- width(prot_aln)[1]
  ci_start <- floor(aa_len * 2 / 7)
  ci_end   <- ceiling(aa_len * 3 / 7)
}
ci_nt_start <- (ceiling(ci_start) - 1) * 3 + 1
ci_nt_end   <- ceiling(ci_end) * 3
cat(sprintf("CI region: aa %d-%d; nt %d-%d\n", ci_start, ci_end, ci_nt_start, ci_nt_end))




12 Extract CI Region


Code
codon_CI <- subseq(codon_aln, start = ci_nt_start, end = ci_nt_end)
writeXStringSet(codon_CI, here::here("data", "potyvirus_CI_codon_alignment.fna"))
cat("CI alignment length =", width(codon_CI)[1], "nt\n")




13 Motif Definition and Extraction
Define your motif below and extract its presence/position.


Code
motif_nt <- "ATGGACTTCGACGTTGAA"   # 18-nt motif in CI region
motif_aa <- as.character(translate(DNAString(motif_nt)))

find_motif <- function(seq, motif) {
  m <- matchPattern(motif, seq)
  if (length(start(m)) == 0) NA else start(m)[1]
}

motif_pos_nt <- sapply(codon_CI, find_motif, motif = DNAString(motif_nt))
motif_state  <- sapply(names(codon_CI), function(id) {
  seq <- as.character(codon_CI[[id]])
  if (grepl(motif_nt, seq, fixed = TRUE)) "canonical" else "other"
})
motif_df <- tibble(taxon = names(codon_CI), motif = motif_state, pos_nt = motif_pos_nt)
write.csv(motif_df, here::here("data", "CI_motif_states.csv"), row.names = FALSE)




14 Phylogeny on CI Region
Infer the phylogeny using IQ-TREE, or read a precomputed tree.


Code
# Run this command in a terminal to infer the CI tree:
# iqtree2 -s potyvirus_CI_codon_alignment.fna -p iqtree_codon_partitions.txt -m MFP -B 1000 -T AUTO -pre potyvirus_CI
# ci_tree <- read.tree(here::here("data", "potyvirus_CI.treefile"))




15 Visualisation: Trait Mapping


Code
# Load a precomputed CI tree. The IQ-TREE codon tree needs rentrez + IQ-TREE;
# scripts/build_ci_tree.R produces an equivalent offline NJ tree from the local
# CI protein alignment, with tip labels matching data/CI_motif_states.csv.
treefile  <- here::here("data", "potyvirus_CI.treefile")
motif_csv <- here::here("data", "CI_motif_states.csv")

if (file.exists(treefile)) {
  ci_tree <- read.tree(treefile)

  # Trait table: DNAzyme-motif state (precomputed) + genus (from ELM metadata)
  trait <- tibble(taxon = ci_tree$tip.label)
  if (file.exists(motif_csv)) {
    trait <- trait %>%
      dplyr::left_join(readr::read_csv(motif_csv, show_col_types = FALSE),
                       by = "taxon")
  }
  meta_path <- here::here("data", "elm_gget", "region_meta.tsv")
  if (file.exists(meta_path)) {
    trait <- trait %>%
      dplyr::left_join(
        readr::read_tsv(meta_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
          dplyr::select(taxon = name, genus),
        by = "taxon")
  }

  ggtree(ci_tree, layout = "circular", size = 0.25) %<+% trait +
    geom_tippoint(aes(colour = genus), size = 1.4, na.rm = TRUE) +
    ggplot2::scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", na.value = "grey80", name = "Genus") +
    ggtitle(sprintf("CI-region phylogeny (NJ, %d taxa)", ape::Ntip(ci_tree))) +
    theme(legend.position = "right", legend.key.size = unit(8, "pt"),
          legend.text = element_text(size = 6))
} else {
  cat("Tree file not found. Run scripts/build_ci_tree.R (offline) or the",
      "IQ-TREE command in the preceding chunk to generate",
      "data/potyvirus_CI.treefile.")
}





Cylindrical-inclusion (CI) protein phylogeny (circular layout), tips coloured by genus. Genera form coherent clades, providing the backbone against which motif/codon signals are compared in later chapters.






16 Visualisation: Motif Sequence Logo


Code
seqs_with_motif <- lapply(names(codon_CI), function(id) {
  pos    <- motif_pos_nt[id]
  if (is.na(pos)) return(NULL)
  seq_nt <- as.character(codon_CI[[id]])
  as.character(translate(DNAString(substr(seq_nt, pos, pos + 17))))
})
seqs_with_motif <- Filter(Negate(is.null), seqs_with_motif)
if (length(seqs_with_motif) > 0) {
  ggseqlogo(unlist(seqs_with_motif), method = "prob")
} else {
  cat("No motifs found for logo plot.")
}




17 Test for Convergent Evolution


Code
# Requires the codon motif states (motif_df, from the eval:false pipeline above)
# plus geiger::fitDiscrete and caper::phylo.d. Enable once the codon alignment
# and canonical/other motif calls have been regenerated.
trait      <- setNames(as.numeric(motif_df$motif == "canonical"), motif_df$taxon)
trait      <- trait[ci_tree$tip.label]
fit        <- geiger::fitDiscrete(ci_tree, trait, model = "ER")
print(fit)
phylo_sig  <- caper::phylo.d(data.frame(species = names(trait), x = trait), ci_tree, permut = 100)
print(phylo_sig)




18 Summary


Code
summary_tbl <- tibble(
  step  = c("Protein entries", "Reference CDS recovered", "Isolate CDS", "Codon alignment length (nt)"),
  value = c(length(prot_aln), sum(ref_diag$status == "ok"), length(cds_orf), width(codon_aln)[1])
)
print(summary_tbl)




19 Partition Rate Test — Does the CI Region Evolve at a Different Rate?
The rolling-window statistics above show the CI alignment is compositionally uneven, but they don’t test whether the region evolves under different substitution dynamics than the rest of the polyprotein. We test this directly with a bounded-ML partition-rate likelihood-ratio test via phangorn: fix a single tree topology for all 195 taxa (no NNI search — “bounded” in the sense of restricting the parameter space to rates/branch lengths, not re-searching tree space), let the CI region and the rest of the polyprotein each fit their own GTR+Γ+I substitution model (own base frequencies, exchangeabilities, gamma shape — so any compositional difference is already absorbed), and then ask via a nested likelihood-ratio test whether allowing the two partitions an additional, independent overall rate multiplier significantly improves the fit beyond that.


Code
# Recompute the CI window in the FULL codon alignment's coordinates (same
# deterministic logic as "Automatic CI Coordinates Inference" above, kept
# self-contained here since that chunk is eval:false / network-dependent).
codon_aln_full <- readDNAStringSet(here::here("data", "potyvirus_codon_alignment.fna"))

ci_start_p <- NA; ci_end_p <- NA
for (nm in names(prot_ref)) {
  accs <- extract_accessions(nm)
  if (length(accs) == 0) next
  gb_file <- file.path(cache_dir, paste0(accs[1], ".gb"))
  if (!file.exists(gb_file)) next
  rec <- parse_genbank_file(gb_file)[[1]]
  cds_feats <- Filter(function(f) identical(f$type, "CDS"), rec$features)
  ci_feats <- Filter(function(f) {
    prod <- f$qualifiers[["product"]]
    !is.null(prod) && grepl("cylindrical inclusion|\\bci\\b", tolower(prod[1]))
  }, cds_feats)
  if (length(ci_feats) > 0) {
    ci_start_p <- ci_feats[[1]]$start / 3
    ci_end_p   <- ci_feats[[1]]$end / 3
    break
  }
}
if (is.na(ci_start_p) || is.na(ci_end_p)) {
  aa_len_p   <- width(prot_aln)[1]
  ci_start_p <- floor(aa_len_p * 2 / 7)
  ci_end_p   <- ceiling(aa_len_p * 3 / 7)
}
ci_nt_start_p <- (ceiling(ci_start_p) - 1) * 3 + 1
ci_nt_end_p   <- ceiling(ci_end_p) * 3

codon_CI_p   <- subseq(codon_aln_full, start = ci_nt_start_p, end = ci_nt_end_p)
codon_rest_p <- xscat(
  subseq(codon_aln_full, start = 1, end = ci_nt_start_p - 1),
  subseq(codon_aln_full, start = ci_nt_end_p + 1, end = width(codon_aln_full)[1])
)
names(codon_rest_p) <- names(codon_aln_full)

cat(sprintf(
  "CI partition: nt %d-%d (%d nt); rest of polyprotein: %d nt\n",
  ci_nt_start_p, ci_nt_end_p, width(codon_CI_p)[1], width(codon_rest_p)[1]
))


CI partition: nt 5671-8511 (2841 nt); rest of polyprotein: 17016 nt




Code
# Shared NJ backbone for both partitions. p-distance (not a corrected model):
# third-codon-position saturation across this divergent a set makes corrected
# distances blow up to Inf/NA (same issue noted for the NJ trees in nb03), and
# pairwise.deletion=TRUE lets njs cope with an alignment this gappy.
dm_full   <- ape::dist.dna(ape::as.DNAbin(codon_aln_full), model = "raw", pairwise.deletion = TRUE)
tree_full <- ape::multi2di(ape::ladderize(ape::njs(dm_full)))
tree_full$edge.length[tree_full$edge.length < 0] <- 1e-6

dat_CI_p   <- phangorn::as.phyDat(codon_CI_p, type = "DNA")
dat_rest_p <- phangorn::as.phyDat(codon_rest_p, type = "DNA")

# Each partition gets its own GTR+Gamma(4)+Inv fit on the shared tree (no NNI
# -- topology is held fixed; only the substitution model and branch lengths
# are optimised). This absorbs compositional/rate-matrix differences between
# the two partitions before the rate-LRT below asks whether anything is left.
fit_ci_0   <- phangorn::pml(tree_full, dat_CI_p, k = 4, inv = 0.05)
fit_ci_p   <- phangorn::optim.pml(fit_ci_0,
  model = "GTR", optBf = TRUE, optQ = TRUE, optInv = TRUE, optGamma = TRUE,
  optEdge = TRUE, optNni = FALSE, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0)
)

fit_rest_0 <- phangorn::pml(tree_full, dat_rest_p, k = 4, inv = 0.05)
fit_rest_p <- phangorn::optim.pml(fit_rest_0,
  model = "GTR", optBf = TRUE, optQ = TRUE, optInv = TRUE, optGamma = TRUE,
  optEdge = TRUE, optNni = FALSE, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0)
)

fits_p <- list(CI = fit_ci_p, rest = fit_rest_p)

# H0: partitions share edge lengths and rate (rate not in the formula at all
# -> fixed at 1 for both, i.e. no partition-specific rate).
sp0 <- phangorn::pmlPart(edge ~ 1, fits_p, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0, maxit = 5))
# H1: partitions share edge lengths but each gets its own overall rate
# multiplier -- the nested alternative, 1 extra free parameter for 2 partitions.
sp1 <- phangorn::pmlPart(edge ~ rate, fits_p, control = phangorn::pml.control(trace = 0, maxit = 5))

lrt_stat_p <- as.numeric(2 * (sp1$logLik - sp0$logLik))
df_diff_p  <- attr(sp1$logLik, "df") - attr(sp0$logLik, "df")
p_val_p    <- pchisq(lrt_stat_p, df = df_diff_p, lower.tail = FALSE)
rate_ci_p  <- sp1$fits$CI$rate
rate_rest_p<- sp1$fits$rest$rate




Code
tibble(
  quantity = c("logLik H0 (shared rate)", "logLik H1 (partition-specific rate)",
               "LRT statistic", "df", "p-value",
               "CI-region rate (relative)", "Rest-of-polyprotein rate (relative)",
               "Rate ratio (CI / rest)"),
  value = c(sprintf("%.1f", as.numeric(sp0$logLik)), sprintf("%.1f", as.numeric(sp1$logLik)),
            sprintf("%.3f", lrt_stat_p), as.character(df_diff_p), sprintf("%.2e", p_val_p),
            sprintf("%.4f", rate_ci_p), sprintf("%.4f", rate_rest_p),
            sprintf("%.3f", rate_ci_p / rate_rest_p))
)


# A tibble: 8 × 2
  quantity                            value     
  <chr>                               <chr>     
1 logLik H0 (shared rate)             -1610158.8
2 logLik H1 (partition-specific rate) -1610149.9
3 LRT statistic                       17.783    
4 df                                  1         
5 p-value                             2.48e-05  
6 CI-region rate (relative)           1.0555    
7 Rest-of-polyprotein rate (relative) 0.9907    
8 Rate ratio (CI / rest)              1.065     


The likelihood-ratio test rejects the shared-rate null: allowing the CI region its own overall substitution rate (on top of the compositional differences already absorbed by fitting GTR+Γ+I independently per partition) significantly improves the fit. The effect is real but modest in size — the CI region evolves at roughly 7% above the rate of the rest of the polyprotein under the same fixed topology, not an order-of-magnitude outlier.
This should not be over-read as evidence that the CI region is under relaxed purifying selection. Potyvirus genomes are under strong, genome-wide negative selection (Gibbs et al. 2020), and a codon-level dN/dS survey across potyviruses found CI to be, if anything, the most conserved cistron in the genome — the fewest sites under positive selection and the highest concentration of negative-selection sites of any potyviral protein (Nigam et al. 2019). Our test here is a plain nucleotide GTR substitution-rate model, not partitioned by codon position, so it cannot distinguish synonymous from nonsynonymous change; a modestly elevated overall substitution intensity is entirely compatible with strong amino-acid-level conservation if most of the extra substitutions are synonymous. Read together, the two results suggest the CI region may tolerate a somewhat higher background (largely synonymous) substitution rate while remaining one of the more constrained proteins at the amino-acid level — a hypothesis a proper codon-partitioned dN/dS analysis of this alignment (comparing the DNAzyme-motif window, the full CI region, and the rest of the polyprotein) could test directly, but which this simpler rate test cannot adjudicate on its own. As with the rest of this compendium, treat this as one line of hypothesis-generating evidence, not a confirmatory result.






Gibbs, Adrian J., Mohammad Hajizadeh, Kazusato Ohshima, and Roger A. C. Jones. 2020. “The Potyviruses: An Evolutionary Synthesis Is Emerging.” Viruses 12 (2): 132. https://doi.org/10.3390/v12020132.


Nigam, Deepti, Kimberly LaTourrette, Paulo F. N. Souza, and Hernan Garcia-Ruiz. 2019. “Genome-Wide Variation in Potyviruses.” Frontiers in Plant Science 10: 1439. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpls.2019.01439.

title: 3  Codon Usage and DNAzyme-like Motif Architecture
text:
3.3 Predicted DNAzyme Activity
The DNAzyme-like locus matches the catalytic core of the 10-23 RNA-cleaving deoxyribozyme (15 nt, GGCTAGCTACAACGA) (Santoro and Joyce 1997). Whether a given homolog would retain catalytic activity depends on conservation of this core: it is highly intolerant to substitution (Zaborowska et al. 2002), and position G14 is the catalytic general base that activates the 2’-OH nucleophile (Borggräfe et al. 2022; Cramer et al. 2023). Below, each homolog’s best-matching core window is scored against the canonical core with per-position criticality weights (a sequence-based heuristic, not a catalytic-rate measurement).

3.6 Codon Co-occurrence Network
Beyond per-codon frequencies, the architecture of codon usage — which codons tend to sit next to which — carries additional signal (codon-pair bias). The network below treats each sense codon in the DNAzyme-locus window as a node and draws a weighted edge between codons that occur adjacently, pooled across all isolates. Node colour encodes the amino acid, node size the degree, and Louvain communities highlight groups of codons that recur together.

3.10 Advanced Metrics

3.10.1 GC Content by Codon Position











3.10.2 Dinucleotide Bias (CpG suppression)
RNA viruses of plants and animals are frequently depleted in the CpG (and often UpA) dinucleotide relative to what their base composition predicts — a signature associated with evasion of host nucleic-acid immune sensing (Greenbaum et al. 2008; Takata et al. 2017). The odds ratio \(\rho = f_{XY} / (f_X f_Y)\) measures observed-vs-expected dinucleotide frequency: \(\rho < 1\) indicates suppression, \(\rho > 1\) over-representation.





Dinucleotide odds ratios (ρ = observed/expected) across the DNAzyme-matching motif locus. Bars below the dashed line (ρ = 1) are suppressed; CpG is highlighted. Marked CpG suppression is consistent with an immune-evasion signature.

title: 3  Codon Usage and DNAzyme-like Motif Architecture
text:
3.12 Rolling genome-wide composition
A sliding window along the polyprotein alignment maps how compositional bias varies from region to region, rather than as a single genome-wide average. Local dips in GC3 (third-codon-position GC) track translational/codon adaptation, troughs in CpG O/E localise immune-evasion signal to particular genes, and Shannon entropy marks the most conserved (low-entropy) versus most variable segments. Windows are computed with potytools::rolling_alignment_stats() (300 bp window, 30 bp step, codon-aware).

3.12.1 GC, GC3 & entropy





Sliding-window GC, third-position GC (GC3) and Shannon entropy across the polyprotein alignment (300 bp window, 30 bp step). Conserved, compositionally biased regions appear as coordinated GC3 shifts with low entropy.






3.12.2 CpG O/E





Sliding-window CpG observed/expected ratio across the polyprotein. Values below the dashed line (O/E = 1) indicate regional CpG suppression.






3.12.3 Per-isolate GC3





Per-isolate sliding-window GC3, one line per isolate. Tight bundling indicates the compositional profile is conserved across the lineage; local fanning marks isolate-specific variation.

Cramer, Erin R. A., Stefan A. Starcovic, Ryan M. Avey, et al. 2023. “Structure of a 10-23 Deoxyribozyme Exhibiting a Homodimer Conformation.” Communications Chemistry 6: 119. https://doi.org/10.1038/s42004-023-00924-3.


Greenbaum, Benjamin D., Arnold J. Levine, Gyan Bhanot, and Raul Rabadan. 2008. “Patterns of Evolution and Host Gene Mimicry in Influenza and Other RNA Viruses.” PLoS Pathogens 4 (6): e1000079. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.ppat.1000079.

title: 4  Protein Motif Analysis
text:
4.1 Overview
The cylindrical inclusion (CI) helicase carries a short conserved region that, across Potyviridae, overlaps a number of eukaryotic linear motifs (ELMs) — short functional peptide motifs catalogued in the ELM database (Kumar et al. 2024). Here I extract the CI motif-flanking region from each isolate in the ICTV Potyviridae alignment (Inoue-Nagata et al. 2022), search it against ELM, and ask which motifs are characteristic of particular genera.
The ELM search is run with the gget Python package (gget elm), which reproduces the ELM webserver’s two search modes: an ortholog (DIAMOND) search against ELM-annotated proteins and a direct regex search. It runs in a self-contained pixi environment inside WSL; the per-isolate results are written to data/elm_gget/ by scripts/prep_elm_regions.R → scripts/gget_elm_run.py → scripts/viz_elm_motifs.R. This chapter reads those results.
crumbs: Part II — Codon Usage & Motif Architecture

title: 4  Protein Motif Analysis
text: Why a different sequence set here. Chapters 1–3 used my custom collection of the 16 potyvirus isolates that carry the DNAzyme catalytic core. From this chapter on I switch to the premade ICTV Potyviridae alignment (Inoue-Nagata et al. 2022) to extend the motif analysis to the whole family. That alignment does not contain the 16 core-carrying isolates — which is fine here, because I first confirmed the translated catalytic-core region is preserved in ICTV members, and the question now is about the CI protein and its sorting motifs across Potyviridae, not about the DNAzyme core per se.
crumbs: Part II — Codon Usage & Motif Architecture

title: 4  Protein Motif Analysis
text:
4.2 CI motif region
I anchor the CI region on the alignment consensus at the conserved VASYN motif (allowing one mismatch) and take a ±20 aa window. The alignment is read from the local ICTV file (data/OPSR.Poty.Fig3.v16_align.txt).


Code
protaln <- readAAStringSet(file.path(data_dir, "OPSR.Poty.Fig3.v16_align.txt"),
                           format = "fasta")

cons    <- consensusString(protaln, ambiguityMap = "X")
m       <- matchPattern("VASYN", cons, max.mismatch = 1)
anchora <- start(m)[1]
anchorb <- end(m)[1]

region  <- subseq(protaln, anchora - 20, anchorb + 20)   # aligned window
cat(sprintf("VASYN anchor at consensus %d-%d; region columns %d-%d (%d isolates)\n",
            anchora, anchorb, anchora - 20, anchorb + 20, length(region)))

title: 4  Protein Motif Analysis
text:
4.4 ELM search results (gget)
gget elm was run per isolate in ortholog + regex mode. As expected for viral CI proteins, the ortholog search returns nothing (ELM’s ortholog set is eukaryotic), so the regex motif matches carry the signal — the same content the ELM webserver returns for a direct sequence query.


Code
regex_path <- file.path(elm_dir, "elm_regex.tsv")
have_gget  <- file.exists(regex_path)

if (have_gget) {
  elm <- readr::read_tsv(regex_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    dplyr::left_join(dplyr::select(meta, query = name, virus_abbr, genus),
                     by = "query")
  cat(sprintf("%d motif hits across %d isolates and %d ELM motif classes\n",
              nrow(elm), dplyr::n_distinct(elm$query),
              dplyr::n_distinct(elm$ELMIdentifier)))
  elm %>%
    dplyr::count(ELMType, name = "hits") %>%
    dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(hits)) %>%
    knitr::kable(caption = "ELM hits by motif type.")
} else {
  message("data/elm_gget/elm_regex.tsv not found. ",
          "Regenerate with scripts/prep_elm_regions.R -> gget_elm_run.py.")
}

4.4.1 What the motifs are — reference table
Throughout this chapter motifs are labelled by their ELM identifier (e.g. LIG_SH2_STAT5), which is compact but opaque. The table below unpacks every detected class: its functional-site name, ELM type (CLV cleavage, DEG degron, DOC docking, LIG ligand-binding, MOD post-translational modification, TRG targeting), the canonical regular-expression pattern that defines the motif, a brief functional description, and the number of CI-region isolates carrying it. Sort by n_isolates for the near-universal motifs; filter by Type or search the description for a functional class of interest. Each identifier links to its ELM database entry.


Code
elm_ref <- elm %>%
  dplyr::group_by(ELMIdentifier) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    `Functional site` = dplyr::first(FunctionalSiteName),
    Type              = dplyr::first(ELMType),
    `Canonical regex` = dplyr::first(Regex),
    Description       = dplyr::first(Description),
    n_isolates        = dplyr::n_distinct(query),
    .groups = "drop") %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n_isolates)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(ELMIdentifier = sprintf(
    '<a href="http://elm.eu.org/elms/%s" target="_blank">%s</a>',
    ELMIdentifier, ELMIdentifier))

DT::datatable(
  elm_ref, rownames = FALSE, escape = FALSE, filter = "top",
  options = list(pageLength = 12, scrollX = TRUE,
                 columnDefs = list(list(width = "320px", targets = 4))),
  caption = paste("All ELM linear-motif classes detected in the CI motif-flanking",
                  "region, with functional-site name, type, canonical regex,",
                  "description and isolate count. Identifiers link to the ELM",
                  "database."))

title: 4  Protein Motif Analysis
text:
4.5 Motif landscape across isolates
Motif presence per isolate, ELM classes on y, isolate fragments on x, faceted and coloured by genus (each panel is one genus, width proportional to isolate count). The wide bands are motifs shared across essentially all Potyvirus isolates; the narrow genus panels reveal motifs restricted to particular lineages.


Code
pa <- elm %>% dplyr::distinct(query, genus, ELMIdentifier)

genus_sizes <- meta %>% dplyr::count(genus, name = "n_iso") %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n_iso))
isolate_order <- meta %>%
  dplyr::mutate(genus = factor(genus, levels = genus_sizes$genus)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(genus, virus_abbr) %>% dplyr::pull(name)

pa <- pa %>% dplyr::mutate(
  query         = factor(query, levels = isolate_order),
  ELMIdentifier = forcats::fct_rev(factor(ELMIdentifier)),
  genus         = factor(genus, levels = genus_sizes$genus))

ggplot(pa, aes(query, ELMIdentifier, fill = genus)) +
  geom_tile() +
  facet_grid(cols = vars(genus), scales = "free_x", space = "free_x") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "turbo", guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "ELM linear motifs across Potyviridae CI region fragments",
       x = "Isolate fragment (grouped by genus)", y = "ELM motif class") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid = element_blank(), panel.spacing.x = unit(1.5, "pt"),
        strip.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 0, size = 7),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA))





Presence/absence of each ELM motif class (rows) across every CI-region isolate (columns), faceted and coloured by genus. Wide horizontal bands are motifs shared across essentially all Potyvirus isolates; motifs confined to a single narrow genus panel are lineage-restricted.
crumbs: Part II — Codon Usage & Motif Architecture

title: 4  Protein Motif Analysis
text:
4.6 Genus-characteristic motifs
Motifs ranked by how much their prevalence varies across genera (restricted to genera with ≥3 isolates). Because genus sizes are very unequal, each call carries a confidence based on the size of the genus driving it, and a Wilson lower bound on the prevalence — so a motif present in 3/3 isolates of a small genus (point prevalence 1.0, lower bound ≈ 0.44) is not overstated relative to one present in 166/166 Potyvirus isolates (lower bound ≈ 0.98).


Code
spec <- readr::read_tsv(file.path(elm_dir, "elm_motif_specificity.tsv"),
                        show_col_types = FALSE)

MIN_N <- 3; CONF_LOW <- 5; TOP_M <- 30
big_genera <- genus_sizes %>% dplyr::filter(n_iso >= MIN_N) %>% dplyr::pull(genus)

top_motifs <- spec %>% dplyr::slice_max(spec_var, n = TOP_M) %>%
  dplyr::pull(ELMIdentifier)

prev_long <- spec %>%
  dplyr::filter(ELMIdentifier %in% top_motifs) %>%
  dplyr::select(ELMIdentifier, dplyr::all_of(as.character(big_genera))) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(-ELMIdentifier, names_to = "genus", values_to = "prevalence")

lab_map <- genus_sizes %>% dplyr::filter(genus %in% big_genera) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(label = sprintf("%s (n=%d)%s", genus, n_iso,
                                ifelse(n_iso < CONF_LOW, " †", "")))
lm <- setNames(lab_map$label, lab_map$genus)

prev_long <- prev_long %>% dplyr::mutate(
  genus_lab = factor(lm[genus], levels = rev(lm[as.character(big_genera)])),
  ELMIdentifier = factor(ELMIdentifier,
    levels = spec$ELMIdentifier[spec$ELMIdentifier %in% top_motifs]))

lowconf <- spec$ELMIdentifier[spec$confidence == "low"]
prev_long <- prev_long %>%
  dplyr::mutate(lowconf = ELMIdentifier %in% lowconf & prevalence > 0.5)

ggplot(prev_long, aes(ELMIdentifier, genus_lab, fill = prevalence)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "grey92") +
  geom_tile(data = dplyr::filter(prev_long, lowconf),
            colour = "#2b8cbe", linewidth = 0.7, fill = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1),
                       name = "Prevalence") +
  labs(title = "Genus-characteristic ELM motifs",
       caption = paste0("† genus with <", CONF_LOW,
                        " isolates (low-confidence); blue-ringed cells: ",
                        "characteristic call from a small genus."),
       x = "ELM motif class", y = "Genus") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 7, colour = "grey30"),
        panel.grid = element_blank())





Genus-characteristic ELM motifs: tile colour is each motif’s prevalence within a genus (rows = genera with ≥3 isolates, columns = the most cross-genus-variable motifs). Blue-ringed cells flag calls driven by a small genus (low confidence); † marks genera with <5 isolates.




The highest-confidence genus-characteristic calls (largest driving genus). These are the data-selected “interesting” motifs — chosen by an unbiased cross-genus prevalence-variance ranking rather than by hand — so each is shown with its functional-site name and canonical pattern:

                   Regex = dplyr::first(Regex), .groups = "drop")

spec %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(spec_var)) %>%
  dplyr::transmute(ELMIdentifier, top_genus, top_n,
                   top_prev = round(top_prev, 2),

                   confidence, spec_var = round(spec_var, 3)) %>%
  dplyr::slice_head(n = 15) %>%

                               "variance, with functional-site name, canonical",

4.7 Notes

The concatenated-submission workflow (build_concat() + junction-hit flagging) used for the original single ELM webserver query is retained in potytools but is no longer needed here, since gget lets us query each isolate directly.
The gget ELM database footprint is ~3 MB (ELM-curated instances/classes); the larger DIAMOND binary lives inside the disposable pixi env, not in the project.

title: 5  Potyvirus Protein Motifs and Interaction Networks
text:
5.1 Overview
The potyvirus genome is translated as a single polyprotein that is cleaved into ~11 mature proteins, each carrying short functional signature motifs whose substitution changes transmissibility, silencing suppression, replication or movement (Revers and García 2015). This chapter (i) catalogues those motifs with their roles and primary references, (ii) locates the linear ones in the ICTV Potyviridae alignment (Inoue-Nagata et al. 2022), and (iii) draws the curated protein–protein interaction network that centres on the cylindrical inclusion (CI) helicase — the protein carrying the DNAzyme-like locus studied in the earlier chapters.

5.4 Locating linear motifs in the ICTV alignment


Code
aln  <- readAAStringSet(here::here("data", "OPSR.Poty.Fig3.v16_align.txt"))
cons <- as.character(consensusString(aln, ambiguityMap = "X", threshold = 0.4))
cons_nogap <- gsub("-", "", cons)

# Linear motifs searchable directly on the consensus (regex)
scan_set <- dplyr::filter(motifs, pattern != "—")
hits <- scan_set %>%
  dplyr::rowwise() %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    match_pos = list(as.integer(gregexpr(pattern, cons_nogap, perl = TRUE)[[1]])),

    first_pos = ifelse(n_hits > 0, match_pos[1], NA_integer_)) %>%

title: 5  Potyvirus Protein Motifs and Interaction Networks
section: 5.5 Two host-type sorting signals in the CI region
text:
5.5 Two host-type sorting signals in the CI region
The stretch of CI that carries the DNAzyme-like locus (the ELM-rich region anchored on VASYN in Chapter 4) turns out to sit right next to the two canonical endocytic sorting signals that adaptor-protein (AP) complexes read:

a tyrosine-based motif of the YXXΦ type (Φ = a bulky hydrophobic residue), and
immediately beside it, an acidic dileucine signal, [DE]XXXL[LI].

These are not incidental look-alikes. Wu et al. (Wu, Jia, Rui, et al. 2022) showed that the multifunctional CI protein of Turnip mosaic virus contains seven conserved acidic dileucine motifs, that these motifs are recognised by the adaptor complex AP-2, and that alanine substitutions in them reduce AP-2 binding, endosomal targeting and viral replication. In other words, the CI’s dileucine signals are functional endocytic sorting signals, not passengers. The same group further showed that TuMV co-opts the vacuolar sorting receptor VSR4 to deliver its replication vesicles to the endosome for genome replication (Wu, Jia, Ding, et al. 2022), so this endosomal targeting is central to the viral life cycle.
That matters for the adjacent tyrosine motif. Because a functionally-validated dileucine sits immediately next to it, the YXXΦ motif is very likely surface- exposed and accessible for adaptor interactions of its own — and YXXΦ is the classic ligand of the µ subunit of AP complexes (AP-1/2/3/4). Chapter 9 develops why this is interesting: a plant immune regulator (BDA1) is sorted by AP-4 through exactly this kind of tyrosine motif (Huang et al. 2026), so a virus displaying host-type Y/dileucine signals on its CI is positioned to plug into — and potentially subvert — the same endomembrane sorting machinery.

title: 5  Potyvirus Protein Motifs and Interaction Networks
text:
5.6 Protein–protein interaction network
A curated interaction network of the potyvirus proteins, centred on CI, drawn from the CI interactome review (Sorel et al. 2014), the HC-Pro multitasking review (Valli et al. 2018), the movement/PIPO literature (Chung et al. 2008; Xue et al. 2023) and VPg–host factor interactions (Robaglia and Caranta 2006). Host targets are added from the systematic TuMV–Arabidopsis yeast-two-hybrid interaction map (Martínez et al. 2023), in which NIb (the RdRp) was the dominant host hub (245 contacts, including salicylate-dependent immune regulators). Solid edges are curated viral–viral interactions; dashed edges are the high-throughput Y2H host interactions.


Code
viral <- c("P1","HC-Pro","P3","P3N-PIPO","6K1","CI","6K2","VPg","NIa-Pro","NIb","CP")
host  <- tibble::tribble(
  ~id,          ~fn,
  "eIF4E",      "Translation initiation factor (recessive resistance)",
  "NPR1",       "Master regulator of salicylate immunity / SAR",
  "TGA1",       "bZIP transcription factor (SA responses)",
  "SCE1",       "SUMO E2 conjugating enzyme",
  "HSP70",      "Chaperone",
  "CALNEXIN",   "ER chaperone (CNX1)",
  "OBE1",       "OBERON1 PHD-finger transcription factor",
  "SEC22",      "SNARE (ER–Golgi secretion)",
  "PCaP1",      "Plasma-membrane cation-binding protein (movement)",
  "PIP1;3",     "Plasma-membrane aquaporin")

nodes <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  tibble::tibble(id = viral, group = "viral", title = "Viral protein"),
  tibble::tibble(id = host$id, group = "host", title = host$fn))
nodes$label <- nodes$id

edges <- tibble::tribble(
  ~from,     ~to,        ~process,               ~ref,          ~y2h,
  # curated viral-viral core
  "VPg",     "eIF4E",    "translation/resistance","robaglia2006", FALSE,
  "VPg",     "NIb",      "replication",          "revers2015",   FALSE,
  "NIa-Pro", "NIb",      "replication",          "revers2015",   FALSE,
  "CI",      "HC-Pro",   "movement",             "sorel2014",    FALSE,
  "CI",      "CP",       "movement",             "sorel2014",    FALSE,
  "CI",      "P3N-PIPO", "movement",             "chung2008",    FALSE,
  "CI",      "VPg",      "replication",          "sorel2014",    FALSE,
  "HC-Pro",  "HC-Pro",   "self (dimer)",         "valli2018",    FALSE,
  "HC-Pro",  "CP",       "aphid transmission",   "valli2018",    FALSE,
  "CP",      "CP",       "encapsidation",        "revers2015",   FALSE,
  # TuMV-Arabidopsis Y2H interaction map (Martinez et al. 2023)
  "CI",      "eIF4E",    "translation/resistance","martinez2023", TRUE,
  "VPg",     "OBE1",     "host factor",          "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "VPg",     "CALNEXIN", "host factor",          "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "NIb",     "NPR1",     "SA immunity",          "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "NIb",     "TGA1",     "SA immunity",          "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "NIb",     "SCE1",     "SUMO/host factor",     "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "NIb",     "HSP70",    "host factor",          "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "P3N-PIPO","PCaP1",    "movement",             "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "P3N-PIPO","PIP1;3",   "movement",             "martinez2023", TRUE,
  "6K2",     "SEC22",    "secretion",            "martinez2023", TRUE)

edges$color <- dplyr::recode(edges$process,
  "movement" = "#2E86AB", "replication" = "#D62828",
  "translation/resistance" = "#8E44AD", "SA immunity" = "#e67e22",
  "secretion" = "#16a085", .default = "#7f8c8d")
edges$dashes <- edges$y2h
edges$title  <- paste0(edges$process, " [", edges$ref, "]")

visNetwork(nodes, edges, height = "640px", width = "100%") %>%
  visGroups(groupname = "viral", color = "#f5b041") %>%
  visGroups(groupname = "host",  color = "#82e0aa", shape = "diamond") %>%
  visEdges(smooth = FALSE) %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE, nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 42)




Curated potyvirus protein–protein interaction network. Solid edges are viral–viral interactions; dashed edges are experimentally supported viral–host (Y2H) interactions. Node colour distinguishes viral proteins from host factors; hover for each protein’s function. The CI and NIb nodes are the two hubs discussed below.

5  Potyvirus Protein Motifs and Interaction Networks

title: 5  Potyvirus Protein Motifs and Interaction Networks
text:
5.7 Two hubs: CI and NIb
Two viral proteins dominate the network. CI bridges replication (with VPg/NIb) and cell-to-cell movement (with CP, HC-Pro, P3N-PIPO and, via P3N-PIPO, the plasmodesmatal factor PCaP1) (Sorel et al. 2014; Xue et al. 2023) — and it carries the conserved DNAzyme-like locus and the ELM-rich CI region studied elsewhere in this book. NIb, the RdRp, is the dominant host hub in the systematic interaction map (Martínez et al. 2023), contacting salicylate-immunity regulators (NPR1, TGA1) and the SUMO machinery (SCE1) — implicating replication-complex assembly in the direct subversion of SA-dependent defence. Together these two hubs are where the sequence-level (motif/DNAzyme/ELM) and network-level views of the potyvirus proteome converge.

title: 6  Horizontal Gene Transfer Analysis
text:
6.1 Introduction
Horizontal gene transfer (HGT) across kingdoms is the most parsimonious explanation for the conservation pattern identified in Chapter 1 (The Discovery). This chapter quantifies phylogenetic incongruence and characterises the transferred locus using flanking-sequence alignments from eukaryotic genomes.

title: 6  Horizontal Gene Transfer Analysis
text:
6.2 Data


Code
descs <- read_delim(here::here("data", "DescriptionTable_mod.txt"))

descs <- descs %>%
  dplyr::rename(c(Description = `Description `,
                  seq_acc = `Select for downloading or viewing reports`,
                  name = `Scientific Name `,
                  taxid = `Taxid `,
                  acc_len = `Acc. Len `,
                  acc = Accession,
                  query = Query)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    seq_acc = str_replace(seq_acc, "Select seq ", ""),
    Description = case_when(
      str_detect(Description, ", chromosome ") ~
        str_replace(Description, ", chromosome ", " chromosome: "),
      str_detect(Description, " chromosome ") ~
        str_replace(Description, " chromosome ", " chromosome: "),
      str_detect(Description, "chromosome ") ~
        str_replace(Description, "chromosome ", "chromosome: "),
      TRUE ~ Description),
    chromosome = case_when(
      str_detect(Description, "chromosome: ") ~
        str_extract(Description, "chromosome: .*") %>%
        str_replace("chromosome: ", ""),
      TRUE ~ NA_character_),
    misc = case_when(
      str_detect(Description, " genome assembly") ~ "genome assembly",
      str_detect(Description, "cultivar") ~ str_extract(Description, "cultivar.*"),
      str_detect(Description, "isolate")  ~ str_extract(Description, "isolate.*"),
      str_detect(Description, "strain")   ~ str_extract(Description, "strain.*"),

    query_acc  = str_extract(query, "(^.*\\.1)(.*virus)(.*)", group = 1),

6.3.4 Strawberry (Fragaria) — presence/absence of the ~3 kb fragment






NoteWhich virus, and is strawberry even a host?



The strawberry loci here were recovered by BLASTing the DNAzyme-flanking sequence of one specific isolate — NC_003377.1, Maize dwarf mosaic virus (MDMV) — against plant genomes. MDMV is a maize/grass-infecting potyvirus, and strawberry is not a documented host of MDMV. So the Fragaria match is a cross-kingdom sequence homology to the viral flank, not evidence that MDMV infects strawberry. Read the section below as “a genomic fragment in strawberry happens to match the MDMV DNAzyme-flank region”, with the origin of that match (ancient HGT vs coincidence vs a shared host gene) still open.


The viral-homologous insertion in strawberry is a long (~3 kb) fragment, not just the 18-mer. strawberryseqs.fasta holds only fragment-positive Fragaria loci, so on their own they cannot separate a single ancient insertion from repeated transfer or lineage-specific loss. To test this, the homologous ~3 kb locus is retrieved from related Fragaria genomes by BLASTing a representative positive locus against Fragaria on NCBI (scripts/fetch_strawberry_homologs.R, cached to data/strawberry_homologs.fasta + data/strawberry_homolog_table.tsv). For each related genome we record query coverage over the 3 kb locus: near-complete coverage → the fragment is present; coverage only over the flanks → absent (a deletion where the insert sits). Presence/absence is then mapped onto the host phylogeny.


Code
straw <- readDNAStringSet(here::here("data", "strawberryseqs.fasta"))
straw_tb <- tibble(
  species   = str_extract(names(straw), "Fragaria x ananassa|Fragaria [a-z]+"),
  accession = str_extract(names(straw), "[A-Z]{2}[0-9]{6}\\.[0-9]"),
  width     = width(straw), fragment = "present")
names(straw) <- paste0("pos_", straw_tb$accession)
straw_tb %>% dplyr::count(species, name = "n_loci") %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n_loci)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Fragment-POSITIVE Fragaria loci (strawberryseqs.fasta).")

  cat(sprintf(paste("Across %d homologous Fragaria loci: %d present, %d partial,",

Across 15 homologous Fragaria loci: 12 present, 3 partial, 0 flank-only (fragment absent). If the red (present) tips form one clade the ~3 kb insertion is most parsimoniously a single event; interspersed present/absent tips instead imply repeated transfer or lineage-specific loss.


What the broadened BLAST reveals. Searching the ~3 kb positive locus against the whole Rosaceae (scripts/strawberry_broad_blast.R, cached to data/strawberry_broad_blast_hsps.tsv; corroborated by a 250-HSP megablast of 82 Fragaria/Rosaceae subjects in data/strawberry_megablast_hittable.txt) shows the homology is confined to the two ends of the query, with a central gap — exactly the pattern expected if a lineage-specific insert sits between two host-derived flanks:

5′ flank (~bp 1–670): conserved genomic sequence, recovered from Malus domestica and Fragaria chromosomes (~82 % identity).
central ~1.4 kb (~bp 670–2130): no Rosaceae counterpart — the candidate horizontally-acquired / lineage-specific segment.
3′ flank (~bp 2130–3018): the host TCP7 transcription-factor gene, matching TCP7 (and its paralogues) across Fragaria, Rosa, Prunus, Malus, Pyrus and Potentilla.

The many partial (~28–35 % coverage) hits spread across numerous Fragaria chromosomes are TCP-family paralogues matching only the 3′ TCP7 flank, not the insert. The most parsimonious reading is therefore that the ~3 kb element is a host locus (a genomic region fused to TCP7) carrying an insert present only in a subset of Fragaria lineages and absent from otherwise closely related genomes — consistent with a lineage-specific acquisition rather than a universally conserved host feature.


6.3.5 Localizing the insert within the 3 kb (sliding window)
Horizontal acquisition, if any, is expected to sit in a sub-region of the 3 kb, not throughout. Using the combined alignment, we scan along a representative signature-positive locus and, in sliding windows, measure two tracks: conservation among the positive loci and identity to the TCP7 relatives. The host TCP7 region should score high on both; a lineage-specific insert should be conserved among positives but have no counterpart in the relatives (the track drops to zero).


Code
mm      <- as.matrix(as(straw_aln, "DNAStringSet"))   # aligned char matrix
rn      <- rownames(mm)
pos_rows <- grep("^pos_", rn); hom_rows <- grep("^hom_", rn)
ref_row <- pos_rows[1]
refv    <- mm[ref_row, ]
ref_cols <- which(refv != "-")                        # coordinates along the ref locus

frac_match <- function(rows) {
  if (length(rows) == 0) return(rep(NA_real_, ncol(mm)))
  ref_mat <- matrix(refv, nrow = length(rows), ncol = length(refv), byrow = TRUE)
  colMeans(mm[rows, , drop = FALSE] == ref_mat)
}
wavg <- function(x, w = 90) as.numeric(stats::filter(x, rep(1 / w, w), sides = 2))

track <- tibble::tibble(
  pos = seq_along(ref_cols),
  `Conservation among positive loci`  = wavg(frac_match(setdiff(pos_rows, ref_row))[ref_cols]),
  `Identity to TCP7 relatives`        = wavg(frac_match(hom_rows)[ref_cols])) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(-pos, names_to = "track", values_to = "value")

# shade where TCP7 homology is present (host-gene region)
host <- track %>% dplyr::filter(track == "Identity to TCP7 relatives", value > 0.3)
host_span <- if (nrow(host)) range(host$pos) else c(NA, NA)

ggplot(track, aes(pos, value, colour = track)) +
  { if (all(!is.na(host_span)))
      annotate("rect", xmin = host_span[1], xmax = host_span[2], ymin = 0, ymax = 1,
               alpha = 0.12, fill = "#2471a3") } +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, na.rm = TRUE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Conservation among positive loci" = "#c0392b",
                                 "Identity to TCP7 relatives" = "#2471a3"), name = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Sliding-window localization of the strawberry insert",
       subtitle = "Blue shading = host TCP7 region (homology to relatives); the insert is conserved among positives but absent from relatives",
       x = "Position along representative positive locus (bp)", y = "Windowed identity") +
  ylim(0, 1) + cowplot::theme_cowplot() + theme(legend.position = "top")











Code
if (all(!is.na(host_span))) {
  cat(sprintf(paste("The TCP7 host-gene homology maps to ~bp %d-%d of the locus;",
                    "the flanking sequence outside this window is conserved among",
                    "the signature-positive Fragaria loci yet has no counterpart in",
                    "the relatives — i.e. the candidate horizontally-acquired segment",
                    "is localized, not distributed across the whole 3 kb.\n"),
              host_span[1], host_span[2]))
}


The TCP7 host-gene homology maps to ~bp 2124-2973 of the locus; the flanking sequence outside this window is conserved among the signature-positive Fragaria loci yet has no counterpart in the relatives — i.e. the candidate horizontally-acquired segment is localized, not distributed across the whole 3 kb.



6.3.5.1 BLAST hit distribution across the 3 kb locus
The sliding window above uses only the three RefSeq TCP7 mRNAs, which align to the 3′ flank alone. The genomic Rosaceae-wide BLAST gives the fuller picture: below, each row is a hit subject and each segment is one HSP placed at its query coordinates, coloured by subject type. The two homology blocks at the ends — and the empty central band (yellow) — localise the insert directly, reproducing the two-flank pattern seen in a full NCBI megablast (data/…_megablast_hittable.txt).


Code
hsp <- readr::read_tsv(here::here("data", "strawberry_broad_blast_hsps.tsv"),
                       show_col_types = FALSE)
qlen <- 3018
hsp <- hsp %>% dplyr::mutate(
  kind = dplyr::case_when(
    grepl("TCP7", title) ~ "TCP7 gene (3′ flank)",
    grepl("chromosome|genome", title, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "genomic (flank)",
    TRUE ~ "other"),
  # descriptive label: abbreviated organism + feature, accession kept in parens
  org = stringr::str_extract(sub("^PREDICTED: ", "", title), "^[A-Z][a-z]+ [a-z]+"),
  org = dplyr::coalesce(org, organism, "unknown"),
  org_abbr = sub("^([A-Z])[a-z]+ ", "\\1. ", org),
  feat = dplyr::case_when(
    grepl("pseudogene", title) ~ "TCP7 pseudogene",
    grepl("TCP7", title) ~ "TCP7 gene",
    grepl("chromosome", title, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
      sub("[Cc]hromosome:? *", "chr ", stringr::str_extract(title, "[Cc]hromosome:? *[0-9A-Za-z]+")),
    grepl("genome", title, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "genome",
    TRUE ~ "locus"),
  label = sprintf("%s — %s  (%s)", org_abbr, feat, subject))
lev <- hsp %>% dplyr::group_by(label) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(m = min(qstart), .groups = "drop") %>%
  dplyr::arrange(m) %>% dplyr::pull(label)
hsp$label <- factor(hsp$label, levels = rev(lev))

ggplot(hsp) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 670, xmax = 2130, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "#f9e79f", alpha = 0.45) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = qstart, xend = qend, y = label, yend = label,
                   colour = kind, linewidth = pident)) +
  labs(subtitle = "yellow band = central insert (no Rosaceae hit)") +
  scale_linewidth(range = c(1, 3), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("TCP7 gene (3′ flank)" = "#2471a3",
                                 "genomic (flank)" = "#c0392b", "other" = "grey60"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, qlen), expand = c(0.01, 0)) +
  labs(title = "Rosaceae BLAST of the 3 kb strawberry locus",
       x = "Position along the 3 kb query (bp)", y = NULL) +
  cowplot::theme_cowplot() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 6), legend.position = "top")





Rosaceae-wide BLAST of the 3 kb positive locus. Each segment is one HSP at its query coordinates; rows are hit subjects. The 5′ block (~1–670 bp) is conserved genomic sequence (Malus/Fragaria chromosomes), the 3′ block (~2130–3018 bp) is the host TCP7 gene across Rosaceae, and the yellow central band (~670–2130 bp) has no Rosaceae hit — the candidate insert.






Code
pos <- seq_len(qlen)
cnt <- colSums(outer(hsp$qstart, pos, "<=") & outer(hsp$qend, pos, ">="))
covdf <- data.frame(pos = pos, hits = cnt)

ggplot(covdf, aes(pos, hits)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 670, xmax = 2130, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "#f9e79f", alpha = 0.45) +
  geom_area(fill = "#5d6d7e", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Position along the 3 kb query (bp)", y = "BLAST hits") +
  cowplot::theme_cowplot()





Per-position BLAST hit count across the 3 kb query. Two peaks (5′ genomic flank, 3′ TCP7 gene) bracket a central trough with zero hits — the localised candidate insert.










TipProposed next steps (not yet run)





Working hypothesis: if there was any cross-kingdom transfer, it is most parsimoniously a single event propagated through strawberry breeding (the fragment appears in several Fragaria accessions). To test this properly:

Delimit the insert precisely. Pin down the exact boundaries of the central non-TCP7 segment (the candidate insert), rather than the ~670–2130 bp estimate above.
Re-BLAST the whole insert against viruses. The initial search used only the 18-mer. If the fragment is genuinely MDMV-derived, the full insert (not just the 18-mer) should also match the MDMV genome; if it does not, a different (or no) viral origin is more likely.
Fragment-internal phylogeny vs breeding history. Build a tree from the large strawberry fragments alone and ask whether its topology tracks documented Fragaria breeding/domestication — a single-introduction-then- inheritance model predicts congruence.

These need live NCBI BLAST and curated breeding metadata, so they are recorded here as next steps rather than executed.

title: 6  Horizontal Gene Transfer Analysis
text:
6.4 Coordinate utilities
Helper functions for converting NCBI-style FASTA headers to GRanges.


Code
library(GenomicRanges)
library(IRanges)

extract_ncbi_coords <- function(x) {
  nms <- if (methods::is(x, "XStringSet")) names(x) else x
  pattern <- "([A-Z0-9_]+\\.[0-9]+)\\.([+-]):(-?[0-9]+)-(-?[0-9]+)"
  m <- regexec(pattern, nms)
  matches <- regmatches(nms, m)
  do.call(rbind, lapply(matches, function(x) {
    if (length(x) == 5) {
      data.frame(full_name = x[1], seqname = x[2], strand = x[3],
                 start = as.integer(x[4]), end = as.integer(x[5]),
                 stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
    } else {
      data.frame(full_name = paste0(x), seqname = NA, strand = NA,
                 start = NA, end = NA, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
    }
  }))
}

biostring_to_granges <- function(dna) {
  if (!methods::is(dna, "XStringSet"))
    stop("Input must be a DNAStringSet or other XStringSet")
  df <- extract_ncbi_coords(dna)
  gr <- GRanges(
    seqnames = df$seqname,
    ranges   = IRanges(start = df$start, end = df$end),
    strand   = df$strand)
  mcols(gr)$sequence <- dna
  gr
}
