title: 6  Horizontal Gene Transfer Analysis
text:
6.6 Methods

6.6.1 Phylogenetic incongruence test
Two topologies are compared:

Tree from viral CI region (Chapter 2 / IQ-TREE)
Tree from eukaryotic flanking sequences (above)

Robinson-Foulds distance quantifies topological discordance; high RF values support independent insertion events over shared ancestry.


6.6.2 Sequence logo analysis
Sequence logos of the 18nt flanking regions across matched eukaryotic loci reveal conservation patterns consistent with functional constraint post-insertion.


6.6.3 Network-based HGT detection
See Chapter 7 for the multilayer network approach connecting viruses, host plants, and pollinators via GLOBI and GBIF interaction data.

title: 7  Host-Virus Interaction Networks
text:
7.1 Introduction
To check whether interactions between species harbouring viral flanking sequences could explain the distribution of homologous loci, I built multilayer networks connecting:
See also the interactive BioNexus Shiny app which queries GBIF and GLOBI in real time.

title: 7  Host-Virus Interaction Networks
text:
Species–host plant ecological interactions (from EPPO / GLOBI)
Shared-host projections (species sharing a common host)
Phylogenetic relationships (Open Tree of Life)

title: 7  Host-Virus Interaction Networks
text:
7.2 Part 1: Bipartite species–host network


Code
species_hosts <- list(
  "Fallopia convolvulus" = character(0),
  "Luperina nickerlii"   = c("Elytrigia juncea", "Poaceae grasses"),
  "Luperina testacea"    = c("Poaceae grasses", "Dactylis glomerata"),
  "Trichiura crataegi"   = c("Prunus spinosa", "Crataegus", "Salix", "Carpinus"),
  "Sphenella marginata"  = c("Senecio/Jacobaea ragworts")
)

edges <- enframe(species_hosts, name = "species", value = "hosts") %>%
  unnest(hosts, keep_empty = TRUE)

host_nodes <- edges$hosts[!is.na(edges$hosts)]

bip_graph <- graph_from_data_frame(
  d = edges %>% filter(!is.na(hosts)),
  vertices = tibble(
    name = c(names(species_hosts), unique(host_nodes)),
    type = c(
      rep(FALSE, length(species_hosts)),
      rep(TRUE, length(unique(host_nodes)))
    )
  ),
  directed = FALSE
)

stopifnot(!any(is.na(V(bip_graph)$type)))




Code
ggraph(bip_graph, layout = "bipartite") +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point(aes(color = type), size = 6) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 4) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "steelblue", "TRUE" = "darkorange"),
                     labels = c("species", "host plant")) +
  theme_void() +
  ggtitle("Species–Host Bipartite Network")





Bipartite virus–host network: the two node classes (viral species, orange; host plants, blue) occupy the two rows, and an edge joins a virus to a plant it infects. Hubs are generalist viruses (or widely-infected hosts).






Code
proj          <- bipartite_projection(bip_graph)
species_graph <- proj$proj1

ggraph(species_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point(color = "steelblue", size = 8) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 4) +
  theme_void() +
  ggtitle("Species-only Projected Network")





One-mode projection of the bipartite network onto the viral species: two viruses are linked if they share at least one host plant. Edges now represent ecological overlap (shared host range) rather than direct infection.

title: 7  Host-Virus Interaction Networks
text:
8.1 BioNexus Shiny App
The BioNexus v3.0 Shiny application provides an interactive interface for querying GBIF and GLOBI interaction data for arbitrary species lists and constructing multilayer networks with multiplex participation coefficients.
The app lives at shiny/bionexus/app.R. To launch:
shiny::runApp(here::here("shiny", "bionexus"))
Key features:

GBIF occurrence mapping for user-supplied species
GLOBI biotic interaction retrieval
Multilayer network construction (ecological + phylogenetic + shared-host layers)
Multiplex participation coefficient for identifying bridge species
Interactive visualisation via visNetwork


8.1.1 A saved BioNexus network: Zucchini yellow mosaic virus host community
A live Shiny app cannot be embedded in a static document (an interactive shinylive/WASM build is a future option). Instead, the network below is reconstructed from a saved BioNexus session — the GLOBI-derived biotic interaction network built around a Zucchini yellow mosaic virus (a cucurbit potyvirus) host list. Hover a node for its taxonomy and a PhyloPic silhouette, resolved to the most specific rank PhyloPic provides (species → genus → family → order → class) so the outline matches the organism rather than a generic class icon.


Code
sess <- readRDS(here::here("data", "bionexus_zucchini95.rds"))

# base64-embed cached PhyloPic silhouettes (per taxon, most specific rank
# available) into hover tooltips — looked up by node name
phylodir <- here::here("data", "phylopic")
sil_b64 <- function(taxon) {
  if (is.na(taxon) || !nzchar(taxon)) return("")
  f <- file.path(phylodir, paste0(gsub("[^A-Za-z0-9]", "_", taxon), ".png"))
  if (!file.exists(f)) return("")
  sprintf('<img src="data:image/png;base64,%s" height="46" style="float:right;margin-left:8px;">',
          base64enc::base64encode(f))
}

# Keep ONLY directly recorded interactions as EDGES. The saved BioNexus session
# padded the network with heuristically inferred edges (source == "Heuristic",
# e.g. a beetle "pollinating" every plant in the community) and a dense
# statistical "cooccurrence" layer — neither represents an observed interaction,
# so both are dropped as edges. Every queried species is still shown as a NODE
# (including those with no recorded interaction) so the full community background
# is legible at a glance; isolated nodes simply have no GLOBI-recorded biotic link.
biotic <- sess$edges_df %>%
  dplyr::filter(layer != "cooccurrence", source == "GLOBI")

nodes <- sess$nodes_df %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    label = name,
    group = functional_group,
    title = mapply(function(nm, k, cl, or, fa) paste0(
      "<div style='max-width:230px;font-size:12px'>", sil_b64(nm),
      "<b><i>", nm, "</i></b><br><span style='color:#666'>",
      k, " · ", cl, "<br>", or, " · ", fa, "</span></div>"),
      name, kingdom, class, order, family, SIMPLIFY = TRUE))

id_of <- stats::setNames(sess$nodes_df$id, sess$nodes_df$name)
edges <- biotic %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    from = id_of[from_name], to = id_of[to_name],
    title = paste0(layer, " (", confidence, ")"),
    color = dplyr::recode(layer, pollination = "#e67e22", herbivory = "#27ae60",
                          parasitism = "#c0392b", predation = "#8e44ad",
                          .default = "#95a5a6")) %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(from), !is.na(to))

visNetwork(nodes, edges, height = "620px", width = "100%") %>%
  visNodes(size = 12, font = list(size = 13),
           borderWidth = 1, color = list(border = "#ffffff")) %>%
  visEdges(smooth = FALSE, color = list(opacity = 0.45), width = 0.6) %>%
  # Live force-directed physics (spring/bounce) rather than a frozen igraph layout:
  # forceAtlas2 gives a lively but self-settling arrangement; drag a node to see it
  # spring back into place.
  visPhysics(solver = "forceAtlas2Based",
             forceAtlas2Based = list(gravitationalConstant = -60,
                                     centralGravity = 0.012,
                                     springLength = 90, springConstant = 0.08,
                                     damping = 0.4, avoidOverlap = 0.5),
             stabilization = list(enabled = TRUE, iterations = 200),
             maxVelocity = 30, minVelocity = 0.75) %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1, hover = TRUE),
             nodesIdSelection = TRUE, selectedBy = "group") %>%

All 81 queried species are shown as nodes; only the 6 **directly GLOBI-recorded** biotic interactions (herbivory/parasitism/predation layers; edge colour = layer) are drawn as edges. Heuristically inferred edges and the dense statistical co-occurrence layer that padded the original session are **excluded** as they do not represent observed interactions, so most species appear unconnected. That sparseness is itself informative: GLOBI holds only a handful of directly recorded biotic links for this community, consistent with the sporadic rather than continuous connectivity noted in Chapter 1. Silhouettes are fetched once with the rOpenSci rphylopic package (scripts/fetch_phylopics.R) and cached, so the chapter renders offline (Gearty and Jones 2023).

title: 7  Host-Virus Interaction Networks
text:
8.2 Summary
The network analysis places the species harbouring viral-homologous loci within a connected ecological network. Pollinators and their host plants — including known potyvirus hosts — cluster together, consistent with the hypothesis that viral flanking sequences were transferred horizontally via a shared biological vector during co-infection events.






Gearty, William, and Lewis A. Jones. 2023. “Rphylopic: An R Package for Fetching, Transforming, and Visualising PhyloPic Silhouettes.” Methods in Ecology and Evolution 14 (11): 2700–2708. https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.14221.

title: 8  Plant Immune Trafficking Observatory
text:
8.1 Background
Potyvirus infection occurs against the backdrop of highly dynamic plant immune receptor trafficking. Understanding how the vesicle trafficking network responds to infection requires integrating three interconnected pathways:
AP-4 complex (Adaptor Protein Complex 4) is a heterotetrameric TGN-resident complex (subunits AP4B, AP4E, AP4M, AP4S) that mediates clathrin-independent trafficking from the trans-Golgi network to endosomes. Unlike AP-1/2/3, AP-4 operates without a clathrin coat. In Arabidopsis it is experimentally established to sort cargo bearing tyrosine-based (YXXΦ) motifs — including VACUOLAR SORTING RECEPTOR1 (VSR1) and the borate transporter BOR1 — via its µ (AP4M) subunit (Fuji et al. 2016), and AP-4 subunit interactions have been mapped systematically (Wang et al. 2022). Its most conserved cargo is ATG9, a lipid scramblase essential for autophagosome biogenesis (Mattera et al. 2017,).
Crucially for immunity, AP-4 is required for the fusion of the vacuolar and plasma membranes that executes the hypersensitive cell-death response to avirulent bacteria: ap4 mutants fail to fuse and lose resistance to effectors recognised at the plasma membrane (Hatsugai et al. 2018), part of the vacuole-membrane fusion immunity first described by Hatsugai et al. (2009; Hara-Nishimura and Hatsugai 2011). And most directly, AP-4 reads a tyrosine motif on an immune protein: Huang et al. (2026) showed that the AP-4 µ subunit binds the transmembrane immunity component BDA1 through BDA1’s tyrosines Y18/Y257, and that AP-4 is required for SNC2-mediated immunity and for basal, pattern- and effector-triggered immunity — a published precedent for a plant defence protein being AP-4-sorted via a YXXΦ-type signal.
ESCRT pathway (Endosomal Sorting Complexes Required for Transport) mediates ubiquitin-dependent sorting of activated immune receptors (e.g. FLS2, EFR) from early endosomes into intraluminal vesicles (ILVs) of multivesicular bodies (MVBs), ultimately targeting them for vacuolar degradation. ESCRT-I mutation causes FLS2 mis-sorting and attenuated PTI (Spallek et al. 2013). Phosphoinositide PI(3)P on early endosomes and PI(3,5)P₂ on late endosomes/MVBs coordinate ESCRT recruitment and MVB maturation (Simon et al. 2016).
Selective autophagy provides an alternative degradation route and is functionally connected to ESCRT via shared PI(3)P pools and NBR1-mediated cargo recruitment. Autophagy is required for both immune amplification (clearing protein aggregates, eliminating pathogens) and immune termination (receptor turnover) (Üstün et al. 2018; Leary et al. 2018).
The virus works the very same surfaces. This is not a coincidental overlap: positive-strand RNA viruses — potyviruses included — remodel the host endomembrane system into viral replication organelles (VROs), and the same components that run immune trafficking are co-opted to build and protect them (Jovanović et al. 2023). Potyvirus VRO membranes are enriched in PI(3)P and PI(4)P, recruit ATG9 and other autophagy factors, and are shaped by ESCRT and by the proteasomal/lytic pathways — exactly the nodes this model tracks (Jovanović et al. 2023). Concretely, TuMV co-opts the vacuolar sorting receptor VSR4 to target its replication vesicles to the endosome for genome replication (Wu et al. 2022), and its CI protein reaches endosomes via AP-2-read dileucine signals (Chapter 5) — so the viral replication machinery is delivered through precisely the AP-adaptor / PI-lipid / autophagy / ESCRT circuitry modelled below.
The simulation below models steady-state receptor pool dynamics across all three pathways, allowing in silico perturbation of individual components to predict pathological outcomes.

title: 8  Plant Immune Trafficking Observatory
text: By contrast, the assignment of the immune regulators NPR1 and CBP60g as AP-4 cargo — and the idea of AP-4-dependent NPR1 “endosomal processing” before nuclear import — are predictions inferred from mammalian AP-4 biology and sorting-motif analysis, not established plant facts. In particular, no direct experimental AP-4–NPR1 interaction is documented, so no such edge is drawn in the protein-interaction network (Chapter 5); these links are included here only as explicit, testable hypotheses (lower confidence than the ATG9/VSR1 cargo) and are flagged as predicted wherever they appear.

section: 8.3 Receptor fate and the PTI/ETI dichotomy
text:
8.3 Receptor fate and the PTI/ETI dichotomy
The compartment model above encodes a single decision that recurs throughout plant immunity: once an activated receptor is internalised, is it recycled back to the plasma membrane (sustaining signalling) or committed to degradation (attenuating it)? Ubiquitination is the switch — K63-linked chains route cargo into the ESCRT/MVB pathway, from where it is delivered either to the vacuole (degradation) or, via NBR1, to selective autophagy (Spallek et al. 2013; Üstün et al. 2018).






flowchart TD
  A[PRR activation<br/>MAMP/PAMP perception] --> B{Ubiquitinated?}
  B -- No / low --> C[Recycling to PM]
  C --> D[Sustained PTI signalling]
  B -- Yes, K63-Ub --> E[ESCRT sorting into MVB/ILVs]
  E --> F{Degradation route}
  F -- MVB --> G[Vacuolar degradation]
  G --> H[PTI attenuation]
  F -- Autophagy --> I[Selective autophagy<br/>NBR1-mediated]
  I --> J[ETI / HR regulation]




Figure 8.1: Receptor-fate decision tree. Ubiquitination status routes an internalised receptor between recycling (sustained PTI) and ESCRT-mediated degradation, which in turn branches into vacuolar turnover or selective autophagy.





The two branches of immunity differ systematically in how they use this trafficking machinery. Pattern-triggered immunity (PTI), initiated by surface-localised pattern-recognition receptors (PRRs), is fast and relies heavily on endocytosis and recycling; effector-triggered immunity (ETI), initiated by intracellular NLR receptors, is slower, autophagy-dominant, and culminates in the hypersensitive response (HR):

text:
8.4 Pathogen effector strategies
Because trafficking controls the amplitude and duration of immune signalling, pathogen effectors repeatedly target it. The recurrent strategies fall into six classes — each intercepting a different step of the receptor life-cycle mapped above (effectors are illustrative examples from bacterial and oomycete systems):










#
Strategy

The evolutionary logic is an arms race: pathogens secrete effectors to suppress PTI at multiple trafficking steps; plants evolve NLRs that detect those effectors (or the modifications they make to guarded host proteins) and mount ETI. Because NLR trafficking itself tunes ETI amplitude, and autophagy plays the dual role of both suppressing PTI and regulating ETI, the balance between degradation and recycling ultimately sets immune homeostasis.
The network below places these players together: plant proteins are coloured by functional class and pathogen effectors (red) are joined to their host targets by dashed suppression edges. It is a curated, literature-grounded map (hover a node for its class; drag to rearrange), not an exhaustive interactome.




Immune receptor-trafficking network with pathogen-effector targets. Plant nodes coloured by functional class; dashed red edges mark effector suppression of a host target. Curated from the interactions described in this chapter.

title: 8  Plant Immune Trafficking Observatory
text:
8.5 Model assumptions & calculations
The simulation is a deterministic compartment model integrated by explicit Euler steps (Δt = 0.1 min). State variables are pool sizes (arbitrary units) across compartments: plasma membrane (PM), early endosome (EE), MVB/ILVs, autophagosome, vacuole, TGN (ATG9), nucleus (NPR1, CBP60g) and secreted exosomes (normal vs reversed topology), plus PI(3)P on EE and MVB and the signalling outputs ROS/MAPK/callose.
Kinetics. Transport steps are first-order mass action: each flux is the product of a source pool, a rate constant, and the relevant perturbation (\(m\)) and pathogen (\(p\)) modifiers. Writing the compartment pools as \([\mathrm{PM}], [\mathrm{EE}], [\mathrm{MVB}], [\mathrm{Auto}], [\mathrm{Vac}]\), the core system integrated by explicit Euler (\(\Delta t = 0.1\)) is
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[\mathrm{PM}]}{dt}   &= -k_{\text{int}}\,m_{\text{endo}}\,[\mathrm{PM}] + k_{\text{rec}}\,m_{\text{rec}}\,[\mathrm{EE}] + k_{\text{syn}}\,([\mathrm{PM}]_0 - [\mathrm{PM}])\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{EE}]}{dt}   &= k_{\text{int}}m_{\text{endo}}[\mathrm{PM}] - k_{\text{rec}}m_{\text{rec}}[\mathrm{EE}] - \underbrace{k_{\text{ILV}}m_{\text{escrt}}m_{\text{ub}}[\mathrm{EE}]}_{J_{\text{ILV}}} - k_{\text{atg}}m_{\text{auto}}p_{\text{auto}}[\mathrm{EE}]\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{MVB}]}{dt}  &= J_{\text{ILV}} - k_{\text{fus}}\,m_{\text{fus}}\,[\mathrm{MVB}]\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{Auto}]}{dt} &= k_{\text{atg}}m_{\text{auto}}p_{\text{auto}}[\mathrm{EE}] - k_{\text{flux}}\,m_{\text{fus}}\,[\mathrm{Auto}]\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{Vac}]}{dt}  &= k_{\text{fus}}m_{\text{fus}}[\mathrm{MVB}] + k_{\text{flux}}m_{\text{fus}}[\mathrm{Auto}].
\end{aligned}
\]
ESCRT sorting into ILVs (\(J_{\text{ILV}}\)) requires both ubiquitination and ESCRT function, hence the product \(m_{\text{escrt}}m_{\text{ub}}\). The final \(k_{\text{syn}}([\mathrm{PM}]_0-[\mathrm{PM}])\) term is basal receptor resynthesis: the plasma-membrane pool is replenished toward its baseline \([\mathrm{PM}]_0\), so under balanced synthesis and trafficking PM relaxes to a non-zero steady state rather than draining entirely to the vacuole; perturbations then shift that steady state (the read-out of interest). Rate constants (\(k \approx 0.1\)–\(0.5\ \text{min}^{-1}\); see table) are order-of-magnitude estimates from plant endomembrane/autophagy studies, not fitted to data — absolute values are illustrative and only relative trends are meaningful. Signalling outputs are phenomenological, e.g. \([\mathrm{ROS}](t) = 100\,m_{\text{ros}}p_{\text{ros}}\,e^{-t/2}\) for \(t<5\) min, decaying as \([\mathrm{ROS}]\,e^{-k_{\text{ROS}}\Delta t}\) thereafter (the \(\Delta t\)-explicit form makes the decay rate independent of the step size; MAPK, callose analogous).
PI(3)P coupling. PI(3)P is produced on endosomes and consumed in proportion to ILV formation, with a floor at \(0.05\):
\[
\frac{d[\mathrm{PI3P}]_{\mathrm{EE}}}{dt} = a\,m_{\text{lip}} - b\,J_{\text{ILV}},
\qquad [\mathrm{PI3P}] \ge 0.05,
\]
(and analogously on the MVB). Excessive ILV formation therefore depletes PI(3)P, which (a) impairs further ESCRT assembly and (b) raises the membrane-stress term.
AP-4 cargo. ATG9 export and NPR1/CBP60g nuclear import scale with AP-4 function \(m_{\text{ap4}}\); when AP-4 is impaired, ATG9 backs up at the TGN (\(+c\,(1-m_{\text{ap4}})\)) and nuclear import falls: \(\frac{d[\mathrm{NPR1}_n]}{dt} = r_{\text{resp}}\,m_{\text{npr1}}\,m_{\text{ap4}}\), with \(r_{\text{resp}}\) higher under ETI than PTI.
Topology-reversal heuristic. Reversed-topology exosomes accumulate as
\[
\frac{d[\mathrm{exo}_{\text{rev}}]}{dt} = m_{\text{topo}}\,\sigma\,k_{\text{exo}},
\qquad
\sigma = 0.5 + \min\!\left(2,\ \frac{[\mathrm{EE}]+[\mathrm{MVB}]}{40}\right),
\]
where the intrinsic risk \(m_{\text{topo}}\) is set per perturbation from mechanistic reasoning and the stress factor \(\sigma\) rises with cargo backlog. Crucially this term is decoupled from normal ESCRT flux, so aberrant vesiculation (e.g. the AP-4+ESCRT double KO) still generates reversed exosomes. Intrinsic-risk values:

title: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis
text:
9.1 Overview
The interactive trafficking simulation foregrounds AP-4 because it is the least-studied of the four adaptor-protein complexes yet, we argue, a plausible integration point between membrane trafficking and immune signalling. This chapter sets out the mechanistic reasoning and the (largely hypothetical) cargo assignments that motivate the model’s AP-4 arm.
crumbs: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis

title: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis
text: Much of what follows is predictive: it extrapolates from well-characterised mammalian AP-4 biology and from sorting-motif analysis to plant proteins that have not been experimentally validated as AP-4 cargo. Established facts (ATG9A as AP-4 cargo in mammals; VSR1/BOR1 as AP-4 cargo in Arabidopsis; AP-4’s requirement for the hypersensitive response) are cited; predictions are labelled as such and presented as testable hypotheses, not conclusions.

9.2 AP-4: the overlooked trafficking pathway
Adaptor protein complex 4 (AP-4) is a heterotetramer — subunits β4 (AP4B, At5g11490), ε (AP4E, At5g09420), μ4 (AP4M, At4g24550) and σ4 (AP4S, At3g13420) in Arabidopsis. Unlike AP-1/2/3, AP-4 operates without a clathrin coat and mediates export from the trans-Golgi network (TGN) to endosomal and autophagy-related compartments (Hirst et al. 2013; Fuji et al. 2016).

AP-4 is understudied in plants precisely because it is not essential for viability and gives subtle phenotypes — yet in mammals its loss causes a hereditary spastic paraplegia (“AP-4 deficiency syndrome”) traced to missorting of its most conserved cargo, the autophagy lipid-scramblase ATG9A (Mattera et al. 2017; Davies et al. 2018). In Arabidopsis, AP-4 is experimentally established to sort the tyrosine-motif cargoes VACUOLAR SORTING RECEPTOR 1 and the borate transporter BOR1 via its µ subunit, and its loss abolishes the hypersensitive cell-death response to avirulent bacteria (Fuji et al. 2016).

title: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis
text:
9.3 TEPSIN: the AP-4 accessory protein
TEPSIN is an AP-4-interacting protein that stabilises the complex, enhances membrane curvature and concentrates cargo prior to clathrin-independent scission (Hirst et al. 2013). A plant TEPSIN-like protein is present but uncharacterised (Arabidopsis candidate At5g48795). We hypothesise it stabilises AP-4 under the elevated trafficking demand of stress responses, predicting that tepsin mutants phenocopy mild ap4 defects and that TEPSIN–AP-4 association is enhanced during infection. The mechanistically distinctive outcome of TEPSIN (or AP-4) loss is not a clean autophagy block but a dysfunctional one:






flowchart TD
  A[TEPSIN / AP-4 loss] --> B[ATG9 mis-sorted,<br/>accumulates at TGN]
  B --> C[Reduced ATG9 delivery to phagophore]
  C --> D[Impaired autophagosome formation]
  D --> E{ATG8 lipidation still occurs?}
  E -- Yes --> F[Aberrant autophagosomes:<br/>ATG8+ membranes, poor cargo recruitment]
  F --> G[Non-selective autophagy impaired]
  F --> H[Selective NBR1 autophagy compensatorily enhanced]
  F --> I[Chronic autophagy-stress response]




Figure 9.1: Hypothesised consequence of TEPSIN/AP-4 loss: ATG9 mis-sorting yields structurally aberrant, cargo-incompetent autophagosomes rather than a complete autophagy block.
crumbs: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis

title: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis
section: 9.4 NPR1 and CBP60g: trafficking hypotheses for immune regulators
text:
9.4 NPR1 and CBP60g: trafficking hypotheses for immune regulators
NPR1, the master regulator of systemic acquired resistance (SAR), is held as cytoplasmic oligomers that must be reduced to monomers — via thioredoxins and S-nitrosylation at Cys156 — before nuclear import and coactivation of PR genes (Mou et al. 2003; Tada et al. 2008; Kinkema et al. 2000). The compartment in which monomerisation occurs is not fully resolved. We propose (hypothesis) that AP-4 routes NPR1 oligomers through an endosomal compartment whose reducing environment promotes monomerisation before retrograde delivery and nuclear import:






flowchart LR
  A[NPR1 oligomers<br/>cytoplasm] --> B[AP-4-mediated<br/>TGN sorting]
  B --> C[Endosomal compartment<br/>reducing environment]
  C --> D[Monomerisation]
  D --> E[Retrograde to cytosol]
  E --> F[Nuclear import → PR genes]
  B -. AP-4 loss .-> G[Missorted / sequestered NPR1]
  G --> H[Reduced SAR<br/>or constitutive autoimmunity]




Figure 9.2: Hypothesised AP-4-dependent endosomal step in NPR1 activation (predicted, not established). AP-4 loss could either reduce nuclear NPR1 (loss of function) or, if the compartment normally sequesters NPR1, increase it (gain of function/autoimmunity).





This predicts that ap4 mutants show altered NPR1 localisation and SA-response kinetics, testable by ap4 npr1 epistasis. Analogously, CBP60g — a calcium-responsive transcription factor that activates the SA-biosynthesis gene ICS1 (Wang et al. 2009) — may be held in a vesicular pool and released for rapid perinuclear import upon MAMP-triggered Ca²⁺ influx, with AP-4 providing the sorting step; ap4 mutants would then show delayed CBP60g nuclear accumulation and reduced SA after infection.






ImportantA published AP-4 → immunity link — and a hidden thread to NPR1



The NPR1/CBP60g routes above remain predictions. But there is now a concrete, published AP-4↔︎immunity mechanism that makes the whole idea far less speculative. Huang et al. (2026) showed that the AP-4 µ subunit binds the transmembrane immune protein BDA1 through BDA1’s tyrosine residues (Y18, Y257), and that AP-4 is required for SNC2-mediated immunity (a receptor-like-protein pathway) as well as for basal, pattern- and effector-triggered immunity; a second endocytic adaptor, ECA4, tunes BDA1 levels in parallel (Wang et al. 2025). This is a direct precedent for a plant defence protein being AP-4-sorted via a tyrosine-based motif — precisely the motif class the viral CI displays (Chapter 5).
The thread to NPR1 is suggestive but not yet fully characterised: SNC2 is literally SUPPRESSOR OF NPR1-1, CONSTITUTIVE 2 — i.e. it was isolated genetically in relation to NPR1 and defines an RLP-based resistance branch that runs partly parallel to / independent of NPR1. So AP-4, by gating BDA1/SNC2, sits on an immune axis genetically tied to NPR1, without (to my knowledge) a demonstrated direct AP-4–NPR1 interaction. We flag this as a hypothesis worth pursuing, not an established pathway.
crumbs: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis

title: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis
section: 9.5 Cargo missorting as a distinct pathology
text:
9.5 Cargo missorting as a distinct pathology
The central hypothesis reframes AP-4 deficiency: the damaging consequence may be not failed degradation but aberrant localisation, producing a signalling imbalance. Missorting can create autophagy-resistant protein aggregates and mislocalised signalling components — a route to chronic, autoimmune-like activation that is mechanistically distinct from simple loss of function. Several independent routes converge on this outcome, paralleling known plant autoimmune mutants (e.g. ESCRT-defective vps alleles that fail to degrade activated receptors; autophagy-deficient backgrounds that accumulate NLRs):

Failed receptor degradation → receptor persistence at the PM → chronic MAPK.
NLR hyperaccumulation in autophagy-deficient backgrounds → ETI without effector.
Persistent endosomal signalling platforms when ESCRT sorting fails.
Lipid-identity imbalance (PI(3)P/PI(3,5)P₂ dysregulation) → trafficking paralysis and, at the extreme, the membrane-topology inversion tracked by the simulation (Simon et al. 2016).
Cargo missorting → aggregates that resist autophagy and trigger stress signalling.

These mechanisms are why the model reads out an autoimmune-risk signal (reversed-topology exosomes) rather than a simple loss-of-signal, and why the AP-4 arm — despite AP-4’s subtle single-mutant phenotype — is worth modelling.
crumbs: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis

title: 9  AP-4 Trafficking and the Tyrosine-Motif Hypothesis
text:
9.6 Synthesis: a tyrosine-motif hypothesis
Pulling the threads together, the working hypothesis of this compendium is not about DNAzyme catalysis at all — it is about sorting motifs. Four propositions, in decreasing order of support:

The CI’s tyrosine/dileucine motifs let the virus tap host sorting. The potyvirus CI carries functional acidic-dileucine signals read by AP-2 (Wu et al. 2022) and, beside them, an accessible YXXΦ tyrosine motif (Chapter 5). A cytosolic pathogen protein wearing host-type endocytic signals is positioned to co-opt the AP-mediated sorting machinery it needs for replication and movement — and, potentially, to perturb the immune trafficking that runs on the same adaptors.
Tyrosine-based sorting is a deeply conserved, re-used device. YXXΦ (and the related immunoreceptor tyrosine motifs, ITAM/ITIM) govern receptor sorting and signalling from plants to mammals; the same motif logic is repeatedly exploited by human-pathogenic viruses, including RNA viruses and oncoviruses. That AP-4 itself reads a tyrosine motif on the plant immune protein BDA1 (Huang et al. 2026) shows the device is active on both sides of the host–pathogen interface.
Membrane-topology reversal can originate only at the LE/MVB. The late-endosome / multivesicular body is the branch point where sorting, recycling, autophagy and degradation segregate — and the only compartment where vesicles are generated by membrane remodelling away from the cytosol (ILV budding). A defect that scrambles this orchestration is therefore the only plausible origin of the reversed-topology, cytoplasm-facing vesicles the simulation flags as an autoimmune risk. AP-4 is implicated at exactly this hub, from ATG9 delivery for autophagy to the vacuole–PM fusion that executes HR cell death (Hatsugai et al. 2018).
Tyrosine motifs, modified, are read across the whole endomembrane system. The same Y/dileucine signals — and their phospho-/other modifications — feed autophagy (the CI region’s ELM hits include autophagy-related targeting classes; Chapter 4), ESCRT-dependent ILV sorting, and AP-complex recognition. For TuMV in particular, both NPR1-related resistance and autophagy are established parts of the host-defence landscape (Wu et al. 2024), and viral replication itself runs on endomembrane co-option (Jovanović et al. 2023). A fuller review of how these modifications switch a motif between sorting fates is the natural next chapter of this story.

Everything past proposition 1 is explicitly hypothesis; the value of the interactive model is to make those hypotheses quantitative and falsifiable rather than merely narrative.






NoteReact/recharts version (not compatible with the OJS runtime)





The full React component (PlantImmuneTrafficDashboard) with recharts visualisations is preserved in the project history for reference. To enable it, either (a) host it as a standalone React app and embed via <iframe>, or (b) move to a Shiny app (shiny/immu_simu/) with the same simulation logic.

10.3 Software and R packages
All analyses were carried out in R (R Core Team 2026) with Quarto. The work relies heavily on the rOpenSci ecosystem — rentrez for NCBI Entrez access (Winter 2025), rotl for the Open Tree of Life (Michonneau et al. 2026), rphylopic for silhouettes (Gearty and Jones 2025), and crul for HTTP (Chamberlain 2025) — alongside the Bioconductor sequence/tree stack (Biostrings (Pagès et al. 2026), DECIPHER (Wright 2026), msa (Bonatesta et al. 2026), ggtree/treeio (Yu et al. 2026; Yu 2026)), the phylogenetics packages ape (Paradis et al. 2024) and phytools (Revell 2025), codon/sequence tools seqinr (Charif and Lobry 2026) and ggseqlogo (Wagih 2025), the network packages igraph (Csárdi et al. 2026), ggraph (Pedersen 2025) and visNetwork (Almende B.V. and Contributors and Thieurmel 2025), interactive widgets plotly (Sievert et al. 2026), DT (Xie et al. 2025) and r3dmol (Su and Johnston 2021), metacoder (Foster 2026), and the tidyverse (Wickham et al. 2019). Analysis functions specific to this project are collected in the local potytools package (Muedsam 2026). Every package used, with its version, is listed in the session information below, and all software citations are collected in the complete bibliography.
